title: Sparring Partners: Novellas

title: Law of Innocence (A Lincoln Lawyer Novel, 6)
text:
Textbook example of what happens when the money gets good then the well goes dry....a smart author would stray away from political grandstanding for fear of alienating half of his prospective audience and old Connelly would have seen the folly of this strategy but the new Connelly doesn’t .
An engaging story would mitigate his liberal tilt somewhat but this one doesn’t even step into the ring.
About 6 hours into the audiobook I asked myself why I was helping to finance someone who would intellectually insult me and I don’t really have an answer but I can assure you that next time I will look elsewhere for entertainment.....the first name might be Michael but the last name won’t be Connelly!

title: Cemetery Road: A Novel

I’m not a trump supporter, but in I read to learn and escape, to enter into a world different than my own. I really don’t want to know your political beliefs, much less have them thrust upon me.

title: Saturn Run
text: Simply one of the worst books I've read. I am a huge fan of John Sandford.  His writing style is normally well paced, humorous, and engaging. In this novel there is none of that. He writes of a futuristic Earth, of which he tells you nothing.  He does, on the other hand, spend 300 pages describing made up technology. We get a painful thing instruction manual of building a spaceship and its operations during travel. There is NO plot, NO character development (every single one was so flat it made you want to kill them all off), and NO reason to finish the book. I promise, you will not care how it ends. Save your money and buy anything else. I have never been more disappointed in an author. I honestly hope he didn't write this. Perhaps the blame rests on the co-author, Ctein, whomever that is.

title: Betty Crocker Best Bread Machine Cookbook: The Goodness of Homemade Bread the Easy Way (Betty Crocker Cooking)
text:
Bought a used copy. The book does have interesting bread. Bread sticks, sweet base loafs and variations on old favorites from other books.

So far two out of three recipes have inaccurate measurements. Get the newest copy available and hope the recipe is correct. I am very experienced in using the bread machine so I can correct the mistakes. I shouldn’t have to do this. I am going back to my old reliable tomes.

title: The Overton Window
text:
The literary equivalent of toe cheese, this is Hatriot Glenn Beck's lame attempt to capitalize on "tea party" hysteria with his knock off of "The Turner Diaries."

It's been found to cause retardation in lab rats. Avoid it at all costs.

title: A Wanted Man (Jack Reacher)
text: Child's recent novels are so darn inconsistent.  I really enjoyed his previous novel, The Affair.  Intriguing plot and interesting characters.  Of course it followed Reacher in his early days when he was a young whippersnapper.  As reluctant as I am to broach the subject, it may be time for Jack to apply for Social Security.  Wanted Man reminded me a lot of Nothing to Lose where the only thing I lost was my valuable time reading about Reacher walking between Hope and Despair for about 300 pages.  This time instead of walking, he is driving in a car all over Nebraska and Kansas and everything seems to occur at night. Hate to say it but this is a really boring read.  And for the life of me, I still don't understand the threat posed by the terrorists.  Something about banking credits that could be called upon at any point in time. Ludicrous. And let us not forget the importance of blue dots on the doors in the terrorists' hideout. I do agree with one of the other reviewers that in fact Sorenson's character was far more interesting than that of Jack. Anyhow, hope springs eternal and let us maintain faith that Lee gets down to business and puts in some real effort for the next Reacher saga. Caveat emptor.

title: Personal (Jack Reacher)
text: Terrible. I don't think Lee Child even wrote this book. Seems we're getting ripped off everywhere these days.

title: Fifty Shades Of Grey: Book One of the Fifty Shades Trilogy (Fifty Shades of Grey Series, 1)

title: After You: A Novel (Me Before You Trilogy)
text:
This was the biggest regret/disappointment I have had while reading a book. I am so sorry I wasted my time. I can't even begin to say how much I disliked this book. If I could give it 0 or minus stars I would.

Like most others I read 'Me Before You' and fell in love with the stories and the characters, so much I went to the theater multiple times and watched the movie. It was funny, heartbreaking, charming, sad.. everything all rolled up in one. It has turned into an instant favorite for me.

But all great things must end. (***SPOILER ALERT***)
It is so hard to describe what went wrong because with every turn of the page it just got worse, bleaker and made me so frustratingly angry. I read the entire book thinking it would get better, that things would change... but nope.

Let's start with the way this book turned the life of a loving, caring, interesting man, Will Traynor, who brought out love and excitement to Louisa Clark, giving her a chance to start a new life, into a womanizing jerk who was selfish, uncaring and a horrible person. Over and over this author chews him up and spits him out like he is the worst person you would want to meet. It literally turned my stomach.

There there is Louisa. Yes, I do understand the depression she could or would have felt at the loss of someone who she loved and lost, but this story went way beyond that. There was not one ounce of the girl you fell in love with in the first book 'Me Before You'. It was if this person you were reading about was entirely someone different. It was completely opposite. This author lost sight of the original character she created. There was no string of resemblance between the two at all.

Insert Lily "The unknown kid of Will Traynor'. Are you kidding me? So here she comes.. a drunken, drug head who barrels into Louisa's life and practically takes over the entire book. She was apparently a child Will had with another woman and never knew about her birth. She barges in and just takes over like she is the queen of Sheba. Instantly she shows up on Louisa's door step demanding that Louisa take her in, feed her clothe her and runs havoc over Louisa. Not only does she trash her apartment and steal her money and jewelry, Louisa allows her to smoke, drink and do drugs in her apartment while letting the TEENAGER run roughshod over her. If that isn't bad enough, Louisa then joins her in alcoholic and drugs, taking the girl to get drunk and party! Who in their right mind let's a teenager that they have never met barge into their apartment, trash it, steal from them and then join them in drugs and alcohol? This was not as subtle meeting where the girl slyly slips in and deceives her, from day one Lily is demand, rude, and taking whatever she wants from who ever she wants with no remorse, guilt or even a thanks. Total garbage.

Then let's go into the rest of the people this author introduced in this book or brought back. The mother, the father, the sister, Mr. Traynor, Mrs. Traynor, Lily's mother, Everyone of these characters had a disastrous life. Nothing happy, nothing good, right down to good ol' grandpa who was strung out on meds and stressed. The author slowly destroyed everyone's life that entered this book. The only descent people in the book were Sam, the new boyfriend and Jake his nephew. But the author couldn't leave it there. She not only had to give them death to deal with, she had to have Sam Shot, and almost fired for doing things he wasn't supposed to, but poor Jake and his depression over his mother's death and the breakup with his girlfriend. Then.. Lily.. of course we have to give Lily the grand role of the ultimate betrayal of having given a guy a blow job while he took a picture and threatened to post it on FaceBook, blackmailing her into giving him thousands and thousands of dollar. Oh by all means don't forget the man who "rescued' her form the situation!! Oh no, we mustn't forget to turn him into a pervert who now is going to blackmail Lily into sex for helping get her out of the situation she was in. Just throw another log on the fire of how horrible every person in this book can and is.

And back to Louisa... **sigh**. So now she is a depressed frumpy girl who is working at a bar in an airport. The life and sparkle zapped from her eyes. She's drinking and drugging with a teenager and letting the girl steal her money and jewelry, letting the girl destroy the apartment that she had bought with Will money, since of course she was so depressed she couldn't find anything productive to do with her life and move on. She degrades her even further by now having her boss insist that she dress up as and Irish looking servant for the bar. Why don't we just keep kicking everyone while they are down? Then finally about halfway through the book she starts thinking "What would Will do, what would Will want?" At no point an time would have Will Traynor from the first movie every condone or understand what the hell this depressed Louisa Clark was doing.

This was a complete waste of time, and it really put shame to the love and touching story that the author brought out in the book "Me Before You'.
It was as if this author tried everything she could to drag you down into a sadness and depression. It was if she tried everything possible to make you feel dark and tried to illicit that feeling of sadness and bring you to tears by trying to throw every tragedy she could at you right down to having Louisa "accidentally" fall off a the roof of a 5 story building.

This was nothing more than he author making up every horrible thing she could trying to get you to cry and feel wretched. The only thing I am sad about is the time and money I wasted on this book.

Oh.. just so you know.. the new Boyfriend.. the one she is supposed to be in love with the one who helped her see she could love again?... she up and leaves him for a job in New York. The 'chance of a lifetime' job which is caring for an elderly lady who is about to die.

title: Vegan Cooking for Carnivores: Over 125 Recipes So Tasty You Won't Miss the Meat
text: What is up with these Vegan cookbook authors creating recipes with nothing but processed, store bought, already packaged meat? What ever happened to Seitan as a meat alternative, and how to make Seitan at home? These authors are missing the point when creating these cookbooks. People want recipes with ingredients that are simple, reasonable and easy to come by. Making Seitan at home saves you lots of money and its a really versatile meat alternative that you can create to taste like any meat you want, just like tofu. It's almost like these authors are partners with these packaged meat companies, helping them out by putting their products in the cookbooks. I think a lot of these authors are too lazy to do their research and give readers what they are looking for, but yet they jack up the price of their book!

title: Good to Great: Why Some Companies Make the Leap and Others Don't
text:
Collin's Good to Great is full of simplistic and moronic assumptions. E.g. All level 5 companies (the best) have leaders that make the company a Great Company even long after they leave the great companies they created. And level 4 companies cannot maintain their greatness after its great leader leaves. This is moronic thinking.

Here's a sports analogy to shed light on how stupid this concept of a level 5 company is: If a great NFL coach puts together a great sport organization and say wins five Super Bowls and then say three years after the coach retires that the team is no longer any good...then by Collin's definition this sports organization that one five Super Bowls in five years would not be considers a great organizaiton.

Anyway, by definition a best seller has to appeal to the intellect of the masses...people with average and below average intellects. So, by definition only people with average or below average intellects would think this book is any good.

Read the review by H. James Madigan, "Not Good nor Great". He explains in detail why this book is soooo bad. Even some of the "masses" should be convinced by H. Jame Madigan's review that this book is very weak.

title: The Book Thief
text:
How to describe this book is almost impossible. It was assigned to my 8th grade neighbor to teach the Holocaust. The study material was lifted verbatim off the internet. Of course the students quickly found the site and all made good grades, with help from an internet analysis site. A book has failed miserably when it has to have an expanatory guide. Not one of the students I talked to understood the book. It is a small school (200 students in nine grade levels) and has an exemplary rating from the state. A note on the teacher. I have known her several years and her intellectual ability is rather limited. There is no way she could have read and understood this book.
I have been reading for 70 years and this book is one of the worst I ever read.It's convoluted what-ever--passes-as-a-plot makes a Russian novel an easy read. It is as if the author released the proverbial monkeys on a typewriter and instead of Shakespeare got this book. Many of it's factoids about the Nazi Party and actual historical events are wrong. It's anti-American bias in several places is rather surprising. So many of the episodes (about the only way to describe the narrative) leaves the reader turning pages looking for beginnings or endings. It never quite completes an episode.
I have read many novels of this ilk (and answered questions on them) in a matter of an hour or so. This book took me more than three hours of slowly slogging through it trying to make sense of it and get an understanding of what the author was trying to accomplish. I agree with the students that I needed mind bleach after reading it.
Now for the clever "artsy" lay out and page numbering of the physical book. The page numbering was a mix of sizes and styles of fonts, some page numbers being totally undecipherable. The font of the printed page was hard on the eye and not easy to read. The question sheets for the assignment, lifted directly off the internet, gave the page numbers of the questions to be considered and had many wrong.
The novel was assigned as the sole study material for teaching about the Holocaust. I have known people of many nationalities who survived that war, so a novel is always suspect of bending the truth to fit the plot or bias of the author. This author does an excellent job of bending that truth.
Overall this book left the students with no real knowledge of the Holocaust or World War II. I wish I hadn't read it.

title: The Fault in Our Stars

title: Trial on Mount Koya: A Hiro Hattori Novel (6) (A Shinobi Mystery)
text:
TRIAL ON MOUNT KOYA: A HIRO HATTORI NOVEL (A SHINOBI MYSTERY) is the sixth “Hiro Hattori” mystery, and it definitely does NOT work as a standalone. As a first-time series reader, I initially thought that the novel was a bad translation from a Japanese original. (It isn’t--the author is a California attorney who has a degree in Asian studies from Tufts University.)

The opening chapters have so many unfamiliar foreign words and names (e.g., nyonindo, koya, Myo-in, shinobi, ryu, Iga, and many more), and so much description of special features of Japanese mountain temple interiors and exteriors, I couldn’t find the story that was supposedly there. The mystery of why two mismatched travellers (a Jesuit priest and a Shinobi assassin) would be traveling together to deliver some sort of life-and-death message, while carrying a cat named “Gato” (seriously!) in a wicker basket, wasn’t enough to sustain my interest.

To further complicate matters, this is a historical mystery series--a series which attempts to “remain faithful to Shingon Buddhist architecture, doctrine, life, and practice as it existed on (Mount) Koya during the sixteenth century.” Lacking a college degree in Asian studies, I cannot offer any opinion on how well the author has succeeded in portraying 16th century Shingon practice in Japan.

title: Heaven is for Real: A Little Boy's Astounding Story of His Trip to Heaven and Back
text:
For those of you who are going to rant and chastise me for being judgmental, save it...

I, too, have had a terrifying experience where my child was extremely sick, doctors could not diagnose him for a couple of days, he went into the hospital, and when he was finally diagnosed was not expected to live. This is where my 'judgmentalism' comes from...

Thinking I was going to be buying a sweet little, uplifting tale that would resonate with me as I am a person of great faith and unfathomable love and thankfulness to God _ I came to a point in this book (about midway through Chapter 6) where I had to quit reading this book. The more I read (as written by the little boy's father) about the actions of these parents before actually seeking medical attention for their obviously sick child, the more judgmental I became. I don't like it when I find myself judging people, so the best thing for me to do was to delete the book from my Kindle and just forget about it. If I could ask for my money back, so that no money goes into the hands of people who acted so ignorantly, I would.

Here are a few examples of their behavior that just stupified me: When little Colton first became sick, before a trip, his mother took him to the doctor and the doctor wrote his illness off as a stomach flu. The parents prayed not that their sweet little 3 year old get better, but that he would get better enough not to interrupt a trip. (The trip was for a district church denomination meeting.) So, little Colton seems to be back to his old self the next day, so they go on their trip. While out on their trip, both of their children become sick one night and the parents believed there was a revisitation of the stomach flu. That's understandable. However, when the 6 year old daughter who only threw up a couple of times overnight and the 3 year old continues to vomit "hourly", without any sign of improvement whatsoever, do they check-out of the hotel and take him to the ER or head back home? Nope, they take him to the home of some friends (let's just give this virus to everybody we know) so the mother can take care of him while the father attends church with one of the friends. Ignorant, selfish call I think. But, we're all entitled to a dumb move now and then. So, I keep reading. Once church is over, the father and his friend come home to find the 3 year old still very sick and vomiting "profusely". The male friend the father went to church with that morning is concerned, thinking the symptoms might equal appendicitis. The father, whose experience as a pastor and garage door salesman make him an expert, decides that it's not appendicitis. Fine, anybody can be wrong. Keep reading... so dad decides it's not appendicitis and must still be the (contagious) stomach flu, therefore the family will just stay another night with their generous hosts - just in case they haven't made them sick yet, I guess. The following morning,after a night of the little boy STILL vomiting, the parents pack up to go home and their host, seeing the sick child cradled in his mother's arms, says that the little boy looks "pretty sick" and suggests that the parents take him straight away to the ER. Well, the parents reason that the 3 hours they would sit in an ER would be better spent driving home, so they head on home. They call ahead to their local doctor, make an afternoon appointment, and before heading out explain their reasoning to their host. The host "said he understood", but the father "could tell he was still worried". Okay, even now the friends of the parents are apparently thinking "what are you DOING?". Two hours into the drive, after the parents have had to stop and change clothing on an already fully potty trained child (!!!) whose began soiling himself and the child is by now "crying constantly" and they've had to stop "every 30 mintues" for him to throw up. So, they're still an hour or so from home, and they STILL DON'T STOP AT AN ER! I mean, come on people, it doesn't take a triple digit IQ to figure out at this point that 48 hours of hourly to half-hourly vomiting by a 3 year old can result in dehydration so severe that he could be having organ problems. Wait, the dad even says that 2 hours into this 3 hour trip home that they know he must be getting dehydrated (ya think?!?) and they STILL DIDN'T STOP. So, they get back to their hometown in 3 hours and, though earlier in the book Dear Old Dad says they called ahead for an appointment with family doc, when they get home you know what they do? They go to the ER. FINALLY! And when they get to the ER, the kid is so sick, they don't make the family wait the dreaded 3 hours that the parents had speculated about earlier, no, one look at the child and the ER staff immediately takes them back. Blood work is performed, Xrays are performed, and IVs are run. Results... the doctor doesn't know what's wrong with the child, but the Xray shows 3 masses in his stomach. While the IVs and antibiotics (antibiotics... they don't give those for stomach virus) are dripping, friends begin streaming in. One friend suggests that the parents should have the boy transported to Denver Children's (ya know, since doctors at Podunk Hospital don't know what's wrong). The parents dismissed this, instead deciding (I suppose) to let the Podunk docs google until they make a diagnosis. On day two, after the boy is STILL throwing up and only getting worse "faster", mother stays on at the hospital while Dad works and prays. Finally, on day 3 at Podunk, it dawns on the parents of little Colton (whose looking like death to his parents)that maybe they should take him to another hospital. Denver Children's? Noooo, that would be too far from the parents "base of support". ARE YOU KIDDING ME? WHAT ABOUT COLTON'S MEDICAL SUPPORT? This is where I quit reading. I know from the photo on the cover that little Colton survived and is doing well (he's obviously not 3 anymore). But I just, at this point, had formed such an unfavorable opinion of these parents selfishness or ignorance or both, that I just didn't want to read anymore. As a parent, I love to the point I would die for my kid. Sitting in an ER 3 hours from home is a far cry from death. Subjecting him to days of continual degradation of health and wellbeing so that I can be close to those who will pat my back and tell me it's all gonna be alright is not my idea of taking care of my child. I'm sorry if this feeling is offensive, but it's why I could not finish this book.

title: Haiku Knits: 25 Serenely Beautiful Patterns Inspired by Japanese Design
text:
The book itself is ok. Pretty pics, appealing layout, decipherable text. The hardcover volume stays open where you need it so you could actually work from it. All patterns with one exception are easy and can be worked by beginners.

Sadly, out of the 25 patterns in the book only three are inspired, original, appealing and even wearable for us common people who are not teenage supermodels. The rest is somewhere between boring to tears (to be found in any old knitting magazine) and downright ugly. The patterns are developed for expensive speciality yarns which are not easily found, no usable inspiration is provided how these yarns could be substituted. The "Japanese flavor" is provided by five haikus strewn in the book, and that's it. I guess somebody along the book production chain wanted to cash in on the current popularity of haikus.

This book has to be one of the most disappointing knitting books of the decade, it wasn't worth the wait nor the money. If you want Japanese inspired check Vicki Square's Knit Kimono (cited as source for the haiku book). [...]. If you want haikus, amazon has better books than this.

title: The Secret
text:
The bad news: this book in my opinion, it pure nonsense!
The good news: I only paid $1.00 for it at a book sale.

I will say that both the book and the dvd were very well marketed, and even promoted by OPRAH. To me, it appears that Byrne did not even write the book, but merely edited it. She quotes from over 20 different modern day teachers who have supposedly achieved wealth and happiness.

What is The Secret? Rhonda Byrne says that it is The Law of Attraction ---whatever is going on in your mind--- if the thoughts are positive, those thoughts will be like a magnet that will pull everything good to you.

The process:

* Ask, Believe, Receive
* Want more money, find the perfect mate, have a new house, new car, or huge raise: ask for it, believe you're going to get, and you'll get it --yeah right!
* You can create your own perfect health and eternal youth --what about genetics?

Conversely, all bad things happen because you dwell on the thought too much.
According to Byrne - we get in life what we think about, because our mind sends messages to the Universe.

So is that how some children fall victim to abuse? They thought about it too much, or is that why some people get cancer? They thought about it too much? How crazy is that? If all we had to do was ASK for a cure for cancer BELIEVE it would happen and then RECEIVE it, doesn't it seem like many many people would have been miraculously cured, and we would hear about it.

Sadly, there are no magic answers to why some things happen in life. "Wishing doesn't make it so", and if you wished for something and it did not happen, then the author tells us it's because we really did not BELIEVE it would happen!

Very disappointing. Save your money, and borrow this book from the library if you still want to read it.

title: Crash Proof: How to Profit From the Coming Economic Collapse
text: Don't buy this book and don't listen to this clown. I did and I lost a ton of money. Peter Schiff has only 3 goals: get himself on TV, sell books and get you to invest with his horrible brokerage firm Europacific. Yes, he called the downturn right, but he is what the industry calls a perma-bear. He's been a bear for years and eventually (in any market) it comes true. He promotes his "safe" picks in his book and it all sounds great, I bought it all and invested with Europacific. The reality he is speculating on commodities and gold and he looks great when they're hot but in this market all of my Europacific stocks went down much, much further than my index fund/etf portfolios. Europacific's motto is "There is a Bull Market Somewhere", the hell there is. After the crash I went to see him at the money show and he said in a speech "I don't care how much the markets go down, I'm buying South African gold mining stocks in my own portfolio", are you kidding me! I've been investing for years. Stick with Paul Merriman's index portfolios. They are based on the premise that none of these idiots has any idea whats going to happen with stocks. [...]  and read some of the articles.

title: Be Kind (Be Kind, 1)
text: Although the written story has a good message, the art is biased and stereotypical. White girl showing kindness to a child of darker skin color while all the other kids make fun of the unfortunate child. Are children of color the only ones who are picked on? Likewise are all boys insensitive? The characters would be less stereotyped if they were depicted in animal or other non-human form as gender and skin color neutral.  Next time please consider the illustrations more objectively.

title: The Disappeared (A Joe Pickett Novel)
text: CJ Box is one of my top five authors.  I have enjoyed all of the Joe Pickett stories BUT this one was ruined by its awful ending.  WTH??  So is the author now going to be doing “to be continued”  Very disappointed.

title: The Orphan's Tale: A Novel
text: Why did I finish it? I should’ve given up about 1/3 of the way in. Sometimes you keep hoping story will get better. Not so in this case.

title: Creative Haven Butterflies Color by Number Coloring Book (Creative Haven Coloring Books)
text: there are actually a lot of numbers missing from the pages if I wanted to guess colors I would not have ordered a color by number book what a joke

title: No Crumbs Left: Whole30 Endorsed, Recipes for Everyday Food Made Marvelous
text: I have no idea why they say this cookbook is 'whole 30 endorsed' when there are recipes with non compliant ingredients like all-purpose flour and cream cheese. If you are looking for a healthy paleo/ w30 cookbook, this is NOT it. Returned.

title: Explosive Eighteen: A Stephanie Plum Novel (Stephanie Plum Novels)
text: I was disappointed from the get-go.  These books are not as good as they used to be, and that is because Evanovich's DAUGHTER is writing these books.  Also disappointing is that once again, the book is very short.  The books USED to be worth what I paid for them. Don't waste your money.  Then maybe JE will start fixing the problem.

title: What You Wish For
text:
I'm a huge fan of Mark Edwards but this book wasn't my 'cup of tea'. I felt no connection to any of the characters (except the cat) and didn't really care what happened to anyone. I actually was happy when I arrived at the final page of the book. It was finally over.

While this book wasn't for me, I WILL continue to read Mark Edwards! I'm a huge fan and remain that way despite not liking this book at all.

title: TUNISIAN Crochet - Vol. 1: Basic & Textured Stitches (TUNISIAN Crochet Stitches)
text: I bought this as a gift. I gave it yesterday and when the person opened it she found that the book only has pages  21 to 36! Needless to say I'm not happy!!

title: The Escape Artist (Zig and Nola, 1)
text: This is the first one of Meltzer's books I've finished, and it was quite a chore.  He writes poor dialogue, insists on meaningless "sound effects" and in general seems to have no sense of how to edit his writing.  I tried a couple before and couldn't get more than 40 pages read before I had to stop.  He just doesn't have it.  None of the characters ring true, and I never care about any of them.  He seems caught between writing children's literature, using "Mothertrucker!" as his protagonist's favorite exclamation, and at other times having Nola say "I don't give a turd."  Then he will let a little profanity come in, but honestly, he can't write violence or action, and I kept waiting for someone to say "Gadzooks!" or "Golly gee."  If you don't like fine writing, convincing characters and dialogue, and don't want to learn a thing about any of the key elements in a book, Meltzer is your guy.  He shows a total disregard for details that mark a well developed plot.  I'm done...  On a scale of 1-10, 1 being the worst, this is a .05....  Maybe.

title: The Shack: Where Tragedy Confronts Eternity
text: This was a book club choice, so not something I would have selected to read on my own.  I would not recommend this book to anyone.

title: The Ultimate Instant Pot cookbook: Foolproof, Quick & Easy 800 Instant Pot Recipes for Beginners and Advanced Users
text: I have no idea if this book is good or not. My dog chewed it up under the bed. I thought he had a rawhide bone w him, so I didn't bother him while I tried to sleep. In the morning I saw a half chewed up book, w a guilty looking dog.

title: Sparring Partners: Novellas
text: Book is downloaded to page 127 then the download file changes to another book starting at page 129. Wierd! How do I get may audible refund. The recording does the same think.

title: Instant Pot Electric Pressure Cooker Cookbook: Easy Recipes for Fast & Healthy Meals
text: There are better cookbooks available.  Disappointed.

title: The God Delusion
text: I just saw the movie Expelled yesterday and Ben Stein interviewed Richard Dawkins that author Richard looked like a idiot. Watch Expelled first and you will not buy a book written by this dork.

title: The Catcher in the Rye
text: I read this book when I was about 14 or 15 and I just re-read it at the age of 48, thinking I probably would get more from it now that I'm older.  I didn't.  I am an educator and I can't help but wonder why in the world this book is on so many recommended reading lists. There are scores of much better written and more worthwhile books.  Try reading "The Perks of Being a Wallflower," for instance.  I can't help but wonder if "Catcher" was part of Salinger's journal at some point.  It sure didn't take any stretch for him to create the plot, setting, or characters in this novel.

title: Best of Adult Coloring Books
text: My least favorite book out of 6 I've bought. Not loving the images. A couple ok ones. I wouldn't recommend it.

title: My First Library : Boxset of 10 Board Books for Kids
text: I got this for a little girl to learn some words. The books are smaller than I expected, but I didn’t read the details to know the size, so that’s on me. However, when they show you a picture of a bundt cake shaped JELLO and call it JELLY, you’re clearly dealing with English as a second language brains that are creating these books. Words and pictures that don’t match up to the main use of a letter is another issue. I should’ve read the reviews. I didn’t think I’d have to do any research on children’s books to purchase. Not worth the money! Just save the cash and walk around with the child and talk about things around the house!

title: The Ultimate Instant Pot cookbook: Foolproof, Quick & Easy 800 Instant Pot Recipes for Beginners and Advanced Users
text: If you just got an Instant Pot or are an inexperience cook, do not buy this.  Have been cooking and using cookbooks and recipes for decades and this is the absolute worst cookbook including those sold by groups who put their favorites in a book like church potluck recipes and such.  It's poorly written.  If an experienced cook sorts through the 800 a recipe may be useful, but if you don't know how to use an Instant pot pass on this.

title: Blood and Thunder: The Epic Story of Kit Carson and the Conquest of the American West
text:
Reading the cover, one would think that it was about Kit Carson, and his role in the development of the west. You would be, like me, terribly wrong.
This disappointing masters thesis is about everything other than Kit Carson and the opening of the west. It's about Pres. Polks sly Manifest Destiny ruse, throwing Native Americans off their land, bullshitting Mexico into believing that they have ben beaten in the war, and unbelieveable amounts of ink and paper regarding some Navaho Chief, and his misadventures in the desert of the southwest.
The author's failed attempt to awe his readers by using difficult language and big, but utterly useless words, shows him to be the sophmoric wannabe as opposed to someone who is supposed to have the ability to create cogent sentences.
All in all, Sides missed the boat on this one. Try again, and try to stick to the outline.
A waste of ink, paper, and money when so many other volumes exist that really address Sides missed points.

title: Harry Potter and the Cursed Child, Parts 1 & 2, Special Rehearsal Edition Script
text: Honestly, I am a long-time Potterhead. These books came out as I was growing up. I aged with the Trio. I stood in line for midnight book releases. I WANTED to like this. I really did! But....I don't. I really did not enjoy it. With it's script format, it was a quick read in a single afternoon. That wasn't an issue for me as I'm familiar with script format. It was the story itself that got to me. It felt like it took all the happiness of the original books and epilogue, pulled it out of you, threw it in the mud, stomped on it, and shoved the mess back in where your fond memories were. It is not fun. It is depressing. It feels more like crappy fanfiction rather than a JK Rowling story. The fact that she is not the sole writer is very clear here. I really wonder how much she contributed herself beyond checking it over and putting her name on it. There were cannon errors and characters acted out of their norm (even given the fact they are now adults). Honestly, if I could Obliviate this from my memory and end with book 7, I'd be happy. It changes your reflection on the original books.

title: The Immortal Life of Henrietta Lacks
text:
I really struggled to read this book. I was expecting the story of Henrietta Lacks and how her cells changed modern medicine. Instead what I got was a story of how the author researched Henrietta Lacks. I really don't care about how she made contact with Henrietta's family. I don't even care about the life of Henrietta's kids. I don't care about how the author made friends with them. I ended up skipping big portions of it because it wasn't relevant at all to what the book was supposed to be about.

I did enjoy the parts that were actually about Henrietta, I enjoyed learning about the HeLa cells and what they were able to contribute. However, I'd guess that less than half the book is actually about that. It was a let down. I was reading for weeks and was just forcing myself to get through it. At one point I thought "finally, I'm getting to the end" but no, the Kindle said I was just 51% through. My recommendation is to either get this at the library or just read about Henrietta on Wikipedia and skip all the author's nonsense about how many times she had to call the family or how she got lost looking for their house.

title: Missing, Presumed: A Novel (Manon Bradshaw)
text:
As the police in this novel slowly learn, it is a mistake to presume anything. When a beautiful connected young woman disappears under mysterious circumstances, the pressure is on to determine exactly what happened. More soap opera than suspense, this tedious novel focuses on three women, Manon, Miriam and Edith. Manon, whose name appropriately means bitter, is a police officer who is desperately seeking Mr. Right. Her biological clock is ticking and despite a career will never feel fulfilled without a man and child. She dates a stream of inappropriate, boring men, frequently sleeping with them on the first date. Essentially a pathetic loser, she jumps at the chance to become involved in the mystery of the missing Edith.Edith is another throwback, a spoiled and uninteresting specimen, who would be unworthy of an intensive manhunt save her parental connections. Miriam, Edith's mother, muses about the sad state of womanhood. As sad combination of stand by your man and Phyllis Schlafly sans the saran wrap greeting, she suppresses her disappointment at the monotony of marriage and family while clinging to a man she really doesn't know and pinning her hopes on her daughter. Narration alternates between Manon, Miriam along with some contributions from Edith, Helena (Edith's friend) and Davy, Manon's police colleague.

I did not care for this book. It was a bore. The female characters are pathetic losers.The essential method is that without marriage and motherhood a woman, not matter how successful, is a failure. Suspense is lacking and the story is tiresome and predictable the police politics are boring. None of the characters is interesting. The happy ending was a disappointment. Overly long and pretentious, this book is a bore. .

title: Elevation
text:
When I read and reviewed Mr. King's previous novel ("The Outsider") I ended up giving it three stars. Yes, it was typical Stephen King fare, but the originality of his plot was called into question by other novels I have read and also by a 1999 movie I have seen. So I called his originality into question -- a valid concern because that has always been among his strongest selling points.

Now "Elevation" comes along and has a plot that is related to his original work published as Richard Bachmann. That novel is titled "Thinner" and was also made into a movie in 1996. "Thinner" is not one of his best efforts BUT it was an original idea and interesting in concept.

My preferences for Mr. King's work has always been with his longer novels, where his writing skills paint vivid pictures with well developed characters and strong plot lines. Even though some of them run 1,000 pages or longer I was still totally absorbed in his stories until the very end. Not many authors can keep your attention that long. So "Elevation" at only 160 pages was a bit of a disappointment.

However, despite the soft jabs lobbed at "the right", there were a few lessons to be learned from reading this novel. There was enough depth in his character development to make them real people and his paucity of words still allowed him to paint an accurate picture of his environment. Yes it came off as a short story, but one with meaning and impact. If you look for it you will find it.

title: THIRTEEN FOR DINNER
text: The means are never discussed, which is rather odd given the characters' curiosity about everything else. Old south, underground railroad, it all rockets along with a lot of drama ... then it's over. Disappointing.

title: Lincoln in the Bardo: A Novel
text: I'm a George Saunders fan, but this was a disappointment.

title: Resistant: A Novel (Dr. Lou Welcome)
text:
What promised to be an exciting medical mystery turned out to be no more than a tiresome waste of paper. A deadly bacteria has been unleashed by a well heeled, ultra conservative terrorist cell. Their goal is to dismantle the social welfare system by destroying faith in America's health care system. Despite having some of the greatest minds in the country on board, it apparently escaped them that virulent strains of bacteria mutate and hence, are hard to control. The illogic of their scheme is lost on them, after all they are the same evil terrorists responsible for the truck bombing of the World Trade Center. Enter recovered addict and all around goody two shoes Dr. Lou Welcome and his sainted friend and sponsor, Cap. When the uber-athlete, Cap, takes a nasty fall, he becomes infected. Lou does what no one else, including the FBI, the NSA and the federal government cannot. He unravels the mystery and brings those damn conservatives down.

To say this book is deathly dull is an understatement. The plot is poor, the writing and dialogue worse. the one dimensional characters inspire neither empathy nor antipathy. It is over laden with scientific jargon. Thrillers need heroes with depth and villains that are worthy counterparts. The evil conservative cell is laughable. The author supposes they are to be despised for their non-populist views and he portrays them as over entitled, wealthy buffoons. They lack the fire, motivation and intelligence to be taken seriously. The gratuitous slaps at Christians are unwarranted and offensive especially when the Islamic characters are portrayed with halos. There are too many characters , little suspense and virtually no engagement. The so called good guys lack common sense, withholding crucial information and bumbling about. The terrorists have no escape plan and take only limited safeguards to secure their identities or headquarters. It is at best a preposterous mess readable by only those immune to poor writing, plotting and characterization. Pass

title: Watercolor With Me in the Forest
text: I bought this book from another retail store and also Watercolor with me in the Ocean. I really really love the concept of these books BUT they should have been printed on better paper. Paper that can absorb a little water and layering and scrubbing.  Some of my projects in these books turned out ok it just depended on what techniques were used. The purpose is to paint inside the book with WATER. So the paper should be better quality. I would have purchased the jungle edition that was released 10/20 but not after I couldn’t even finish 50% of the Forest edition. The Ocean edition seemed to be better than the Forest but they both had issues with the paper the books were printed on.

title: The Silent Wife: A Novel
text: I really didn't like this book. I found that I was totally bored through most of it, for being marketed as a thriller, I wasn't thrilled at all! I hated all of the characters, and there weren't any twist or turns that took me by surprise. I also didn't like the writing. There was so much explanation of everything the characters were doing, but they characters never said very much. It was monotonous and unnecessary. It partly explains why I was so bored throughout the book. I also hated the ending. I felt like it was there to make sense of why the characters acted the way they did, but it didn't. At least not for me. I still hated them all.

title: The Shoemaker's Wife: A Novel
text: This story was very difficult to read. It was more like a young student effort than literature. It could be any immigrant story and badly written.  The transitions from one scene to another were terrible. One moment you were by the lake at a picnic and the next year's later at another unrelated event.  I do not recommend this book.  The shoemaker was far more interesting than his wife.

title: Deep Down: A Jack Reacher Story
text: Didn't think it was much of a book.  It didn't have much mlystery to it.  Just ended without really wrapping up the story.  Would not reccommend it to anyone.

title: Can't Hurt Me: Master Your Mind and Defy the Odds
text: Half way through not sure can finish. All about being angry

title: A Beginner's Guide to Day Trading Online (2nd edition)
text: There are a lot of good books on how to make money day trading.  I think this one is designed to make you a sucker for those who know how to make money in this game.  It encourages getting involved in momentum trades...i.e. getting into trades after a trend emerges, which can lead you to always chasing a stock movement, and generating profits for traders who got in early, and are ready to get out.  If you want, go ahead and read this book, apply it's principles, and be one of the suckers that makes money for the rest of us.

title: The Space Between Before and After
text: This book was so slow paced I could barely finish it, and when there was a part that was fast it was done so quick I didn't understand at all.  It was really hard to read and the characters were hard to get into. The emotions were confusing and it felt fake. Some of the characters were REALLY annoying and I winced whenever I read their name. It also tended to stray from the subject quite a lot. But there were a few things I liked about this book. A few of the characters were admirable and some of the points they touched on in the story were important.

title: Masked Prey (A Prey Novel Book 30)
text:
John Sandford has always been a favorite beach read of mine, dealing with obnoxious miscreants, imposing dark humor in bleak situations, and using a cast of characters who are skilled and work together well. His writing is concise and informative and normally flows around a timely subject of interest to thriller/crime readers.

But like a number of other media elites, Mr. Sandford has chosen to climb onto a public platform and link delusional anarchy with the common man. He has presented an ill-conceived tale condemning a bunch of mentally ill, backwards and ignorant small town people whose extreme views would be ignored in their community. However in John Sandford's story they represent every person who is not politically aligned with his political agenda.

Not too long ago someone commented that the media are no longer "reporters' but instead "commentators". Anyone who watches liberal media gets a fully censored agenda that supports their cause, conveniently omitting the previous gaffes, untruths, and damaging comments presented by their compatriots.

Like many fellow conservatives, I have watched the liberal media continually censor their own comments while twisting the words of conservatives and speculating as to their meaning instead of reporting proven facts. Perhaps their audience should watch Fox News once in a while to see what they're missing. That's assuming of course that Amazon allows this review to be posted.

Please listen to Emerson, Lake and Palmer's "Fanfare To The Common Man".

title: The Antagonist
text:
First of all, I do not consider this an epistolary novel. It is written more as angry journal entries. Gordon Rankin nicknamed Rank, whose life story was stolen and published by Adam, a former college classmate, is livid. He sends emails, though there is just a date and time and nothing about it says email. Also, Adam doesn't respond. So I take issue with it being called an epistolary novel. It is written first person, and explores Rank's life, the good and mostly bad. The spewing of anger and the very detailed entries are more for Rank to look at his life. It was well-written but very heavy and I had to stop reading it. It was too much for me though I am sure it could be appreciated by others. But just be warned, this isn't light and it is a bit slow going. It may pick up but I didn't get further than 100 pages.
Sometimes a book might be great but you just can't read it. This is probably one of those books. Maybe I'll pick it back up at another time.
It is hard to rate a book that is not for me so I didn't finish it, but one I think some will find quite good. So three stars.

title: The Suriname Job (A Case Lee Novel Book 1)
text: Too many pointless side journeys for this to be a well written novel. Sometimes I thought it should have been written for Field and Stream magazine.

title: New Guide to Coloring for Crafts, Adult Coloring Books, and Other Coloristas!: Tips, Tricks, and Techniques for All Skill Levels! (Design Originals) Step-by-Step Lessons & 100 Ready-to-Color Designs
text: Nothing new in this book, I have other coloring guides i like much better.

title: The Checklist Manifesto: How to Get Things Right
text:
Its a very very long read. And it becomes very frsutrating to read long long introductions to random people, their job and then long stories on their use of checklists.
Checklists are a very basic and essential idea for surviving in todays world, so the author could have cut the chase and went straight to describing what an effective checkist looks like.

title: Tile Lock Scrabble,2 to 4 players
text: Really disappointed after using this game this week. We took it on a trip and tried playing in the back seat of our truck. The Scrabble pieces do not stay in the so-called "tile lock squares". Even when we played at a table they got knocked out very easily and are difficult to put back. Will probably not use again so feel like I wasted my money!

title: Watercolor with Me in the Jungle
text: More expensive than the first two books and the paper is awfully. Dots show up on the paper after painting. They went with a cheaper watercolor paper then the first two books.

title: The Flamethrowers: A Novel
text: I ended up DNF'ing this book after about 100 pages. I really wanted to like this book as the reviews have been great. But as with a previous book of Kushner's, Telex From Cuba, I find I can only get so far before giving up. The story of Reno seemed interesting, but the alternate chapters about the Valera family were so boring. And Reno's story just didn't move fast enough. Maybe someday I will pick this back up and just start in the middle of the book and see if it is more enjoyable that way. But not anytime soon, there are too many other books that captivate me much sooner.

title: The Forever Girl
text:
A story of obsession but not very much love or romance or passion. Living with her parents, Amanda and David, in a British community on Grand Cayman Island is Sally who at age 4 decides she likes the name Clover better. At age 6 Sally/Clover falls in love with her best friend, James, also age six. Funny, about the same time Amanda decides she loves James' father. Amanda and David separate for 3 years. Clover goes off to boarding school at age 14. then to college, pining for James for years (unknown to James who says he thinks of her as a sister) she practically lives her life through James by following him across the world to London, Australia, Singapore.

from author of No. 1 Ladies' Detective Agency series, "44 Scotland Street" series & the Isabel Dalhousie novels.

title: A Potty for Me!
text: This book should be a board book because kids of potty training age easily rip the pages. Most of the book is about the kid not wanting to use the potty, which sends to opposite message than I wanted. It's also really boring and gets super old if you read it to your child every other night or so.

title: The Walls
text:
THE WALLS is a rather slow-moving novel about a severely abused wife's inner turmoil regarding staying with her husband (if she leaves, will he carry out his threats to her teenage son and elderly father?), and plotting her ultimate escape (can she use her prison-insider knowledge to plot a murder, and get away with it?).

The “perfect crime” she envisions is mundane, with an imperfect alibi (watching a movie by herself?). The crime also takes a decidedly back seat to her constant thoughts about protecting the two close family members who mean the world to her. They, of course, think the husband is a terrific guy, and are unaware of the abuse.

As a mystery/thriller, the novel falls short. However, it seems accurate enough in portraying a woman who is trapped in an abusive marriage. It isn't always so easy for a woman to call the cops on a husband, or simply to divorce him. In my opinion, the value of this novel lies in its food for thought: Does the abuse inflicted on the heroine by her controlling, abusive husband justify the heroine's choice of escape route? Could she have done anything else? Should she be forgiven?

I ordered this book as a mystery/thriller, and was disappointed when (1) it turned out to be women's literary fiction, not general escape fiction; and (2) the prison setting seemed incidental to the overall plot.

title: Haiku Knits: 25 Serenely Beautiful Patterns Inspired by Japanese Design
text: The yarn choices by the designer were too trendy and outdated.  The patterns themselves had many errors and I spent almost an hour changing them.  Not a very book.  Vicki Squares kimono books were much, much better.

title: The Secret History of Mermaids
text: Not what I expected but would make a cute gift for a mermaid enthusiast. It has a kind of pop out book quality I guess you would call it where you lift these tabs in the book and there are little facts and things underneath.  I don't remember thisi in the description but  maybe I didn't read it thoroughly. It didn't suit what I'd ordered for which was more a decorative use and I have a vintage vibe going there and this book has a modern feel to it. Hope this helps.

title: Listen to the Squawking Chicken: When Mother Knows Best, What's a Daughter To Do? A Memoir (Sort Of)
text:
Unfortunately DNF for me.

I was looking forward to reading, but after the first chapter I put it down and it took me a long time to pick it up again....with the same result.

Where I was expecting funny, I got flat. It was uncomfortable reading this. I didn't feel any affection from the author towards her mother....which made it not funny and just uncomfortable reading.

title: Medical Terminology:The Basics (Quick Study Academic)
text: Your money would be better spent in buying index cards and making your own flash cards based on your textbook.

title: Vintage Classic Coloring Pages: Adult Coloring Book (Relaxing coloring pages, Stress Relieving Designs, People, Animals, Flowers, Fairies and More)
text: I like the front cover that's why I purchased this book - but when I was going thru the pictures inside - half way thru there was a break in the book with totally different pictures not even related to the cover theme - the pictures in the back half of book are really not something I'd want to color. Probably my fault for not paying closer attention the description of book - not sure if I'm going to keep it or return it now

title: Locked In (Jessica Daniel)
text:
Plot: Jessica Daniels, a hard-boiled, foul-mouthed Detective Sergeant in Manchester England, is dealing with her first murder case. All the victims were mysteriously murdered in their own homes but nothing shows forced entry – all the doors and windows were locked and nothing was tampered with. Hence, the meaning of the title.

If you’ve seen the 1986 film “Manhunter” you may already guess how the killer did his work. I was disappointed that I correctly guessed the identity of the killer before I even got halfway through the book.

The character of Jessica also turned me off – she talks like the stereotype of your typical hard-boiled detective without any endearing or memorable qualities.

The story was believably told (lots of police procedural details accurately depicted) but it's just not memorable. I’m surprised it was a bestseller in the UK.

title: Harry Potter and the Cursed Child, Parts 1 & 2, Special Rehearsal Edition Script
text:
Sadly, that sound you hear is the ball being dropped. I didn't realize Rowling didn't actually write this but it sure makes sense after reading it. There are some good things in it, it has the bones of possibly being a decent story, but the characters are so.....out of character. The dialogue is forced and unnatural and most of it just doesn't ring true. And now for specifics that are SPOILERS.......

Voldemort with a daughter? I'm having a hard time wrapping my head around that one. It's way worse than trying to imagine your parents having sex, to be truthful.
And making Harry watch his parents murder? Nope. Would the real Harry Potter have been able to calmly watch that? I think it would have been a lot better for them to leave as Voldemort was going up the walk. I think we all would have been happier.

I'm sad. This had potential but as it is......epic fail.

title: E.R. Nurses: True Stories from America's Greatest Unsung Heroes
text: An Emergency Nurse you can't get on paper. This is more about the pandemic then nursing.

title: Venturess
text:
I really liked MECHANICA, the first book about Nicolette. I liked how Nick saved herself and that the author didn’t go the traditional route with a romance in a YA book. When MECHANICA was published in 2015, I hoped there would be a sequel to continue her story.

I’m sad to give VENTURESS a 2 star rating.

Though I liked getting to see into the successful business Nick set up after the Exposition, I feel like this sequel just wasn’t needed. The book is supposed to be about the journey to Faerie, but that doesn’t even start until page 87. And then the revelations that happened in Faerie … yeah, I wish I hadn’t read this sequel. I would’ve been much happier with my few unanswered questions from MECHANICA rather than learning what I learned in VENTURESS.

title: Thirteen Reasons Why
text: I didn't really like how it was written and I didn't enjoy the plot altogether. Not my kind of book.

title: Uprooted
text: This book was in the romance category, and that's why I choose it, but to be honest I think it was misplaced. It really was more of a fantasy. It's about a girl who is chosen to be the Dragon, or wizards, servant/girl/pupil for a decade. She is described as ridiculously clumsy, always getting dirty. He just seems mean, to be honest. He's always yelling at her and mean. Some bad, dark things happen, which really did not appeal to me such as them having to burn a bunch of poor cows who'd been taken over by dark magic. It just seemed really dark. there certainly did not seem a whole lot of romance to it. The one thing I can say was that the writing was competent. It did flow well and was written professionally. Still, it was not a book for me.

title: Beyond The Veil: A Muse Urban Fantasy (The Veil Series Book 1)
text:
THIS REVIEW IS FOR THE 5 BOOKS IN THE SERIES.

I read the first book as a KU and decided to purchase the rest based on that. I usually don't do that and this series is the perfect example of why not... The first book was great and I took a chance that the rest would follow. Unfortunately, in my opinion, they slowly went downhill with each successive book ending in a disappointing manner. Spoilers below, so don't read unless you want to avoid the frustration of this series.

I will start by saying how much I hated Muse. She was wishy-washy, flip-flopping, couldn't make up her mind, ungrateful, and thought she was less than BUT better than demons AND humans. She was supposed to be a kick-a$$ character, but she needs saving from somebody every other scene and is knocked out innumerable times. She likes to think she sees the world in shades of gray, and mentions so more than once. Yet, she is the same as Adam in thinking that all demons are liars, out for themselves, can't be trusted, murdering, conscienceless beasts. Never mind that she is HALF OF WHAT SHE DESPISES. So is Stefan for that matter, but because they are half-human they are better. Yeah, cuz there have NEVER been self-serving, murderous, lying, psychotic humans...

Another example of Muse's stupidity and prejudicial thinking when it suits her is when she is so worried about the soul-lock with Damian but waits until it's almost too late because having Akil replace Damian's soul-lock with his own would be just as bad. So, because they are both demons, they are the same even though one was a crazy, torturing, child-raping, skin-peeling maniac and the other one rescued her from that hot bloody mess, took care of her, taught her how to be human, watched over her and protected her, and finally sacrificed what he wanted for her. Yup, no difference between them at all, they are equal in all ways. Know how we know? Because they are both demons. It was things like this that made me grit my teeth in frustration. But unless there is truly something grossly objectionable, I finish my dang books!

Muse and her bigotry irritated me enough that when I was halfway through book 4 I had to skip to the end of book 5 to see if she got what she deserved. In a way she did as she was, in my mind, denied the BETTER demon. Yup, demon because there is my other gripe; Akil may be a demon but Stefan is HALF DEMON AND WAS A DEMON PRINCE ALSO!!! Stefan TOLD her to her face MORE THAN ONCE that he wanted to kill her, but he didn't really mean it, because hey, he was a human like her (eye roll).

Muse was shallow, self-centered, uncaring when she hurt others (if they weren't *human*), prejudiced, bigoted, and never grew as a person. However, Akil DID grow as a character and was the better "person". If I had to decide who cared more based on actions, it would have been Akil. But, whatevs, immature Muse didn't deserve that devotion anyway. I also disagree with other reviewers who say they understand why the series ends the way it does as nothing else would have worked. It was the author's world, she could have made anything work had she wanted it to.

So, 2.5 stars because of the things I did like; Akil, Jerry, the world building, and any author's talent because I could not write a book let alone multiple books. And, many, many, MANY negative stars for selfish, immature Muse. I didn't like Stefan or Ryder, either. I felt both they were all very one dimensional characters. Hmm, there;'s a theme here, the DEMONS were interesting, the humans, not so much. I'm just thankful I'm finished the series and will not read anymore by this author.

title: Stained Glass Coloring Book: Flower Designs
text:
Disappointed. The paper isn’t great at all which isn’t that big of a deal because i usually copy onto watercolor paper anyway but Inpurchased this book specifically for the image displayed in the cover and inside the hook the image is not only backwards but missing the border which is exactly what I wanted it for OF COURSE so that figures. All of the images appear backwards compared to what was shown and that’s just really strange to me:
I may return this book I may not I’m not sure yet I’m really upset the image I wanted isn’t even actually in the book. Who puts images on the cover of a coloring book that AREN’T even inside the book anywhere!! That’s so messed up!

title: House Rules: A Novel
text: I have read, or tried to read, every one of Jodi Picoult's books.  Only one other did I have to put down out of sheer boredom.  This one was such a disappointment.  Maybe I've just read too many of her books, they've become too predictable, I don't know, but I read a third of the way, then flipped to the very end and read the last case study, which told me everything I already had figured out.

title: The Knitter's Dictionary: Knitting Know-How from A to Z
text:
What a disappointment--from Interweave Press, of all publishers! THE KNITTER’S DICTIONARY: KNITTING KNOW-HOW FROM A TO Z is a small, handbook-sized dictionary of knitting terms that has mediocre-to-poor definitions. It's spottily illustrated with line drawings, but all it really does is define specialized terms that you may encounter in knitting patterns or magazines. It's far from a solid knitting-bag reference, such as Vicki Square’s classic [[ASIN:1596683147 Knitter’s Companion]] (which defines many of the same terms).

For example, in this book the un-illustrated definition of “combination knitting” reads: “A variation of Continental-style knitting. Yarn is wrapped the other way around for the purl stitch so that the stitches are mounted left leg forward on the needle. Knit stitches are then worked through the back loop. …” There is an illustration for “purling Continental-style”, and another for “knitting through the back loop”, but they don't really help me to visualize combination knitting. I know what “the other way around” means, but I wouldn't know if I needed a definition of this term.

As another example, “needle gauge” is defined--without an illustration--as “Tool for measuring the size of a needle.” (That's really helpful.) Or look at the entry for “hat styles”, which has useful illustrations with useless definitions. “Beanie” is defined as “A style of hat, close-fitted to the head”; “toque” is defined as “Common Canadian usage, a fitted hat like a beanie. May or may not have a pom-pom.” (Is there any difference?)

This un-illustrated definition, for “Cowichan, curling sweater” falls into the “why bother?” category. It reads: “These are both styles of a heavy knit jacket, typically worked in very thick yarn, featuring a shawl collar and intarsia colorwork designs. This style of garment originated in indigenous communities in the Pacific Northwest; the garment known as curling or Mary Maxim sweaters borrow the basic concept for fashion.” I know what a Cowichan sweater looks like, but have never heard of a “Mary Maxim sweater”. I’m familiar with the Mary Maxim crafts catalog, but am consumed by curiosity about what a Cowichan-inspired, fashionable “Mary Maxim sweater” might look like.

This is a knitting book that didn't need to be published. There are many far better knitting references already available.

title: Infinite Powers: How Calculus Reveals the Secrets of the Universe
text: Calculus is widely perceived as important part of science in understanding basic laws of physics. But it also has important applications in advanced physics; relativity and quantum mechanics, cosmology, astronomy, biology, chemistry, medicine, geology, ecology and in everyday life. In this book, the author discusses calculus as catch-as-catch-can story in an historical context without giving some ideas of how calculus helped physics to evolve. This is not a recipe book and at the same time it is not overwhelming. But in the absence of clear mathematical methods or its applications, this is a slapdash story that does not make calculus interesting!

title: This Is the Water: A Novel (P.S.)
text: The most important thing I can probably say about this novel is that I found the writing style irritating.  The book is written in the second person, and a disturbingly large number of the paragraphs begin with "This is..."  (This is the pool, this is the car, this is Chris, blah, blah, blah).  There seems to be absolutely nothing gained from this choice, and quite a bit lost (like the reader's sanity).  Other reviewers have mentioned gimmicks, and I have to agree.  There's no benefit to this, just a shrieking attempt to be hip and scream "notice me! notice me!"  When you get past the stylistic choices, the characters are also quite annoying.  This is a novel about an elite high school swim team, and as with any high-level kids' sport, you're going to have plenty of self-centered and annoying people involved.  The author's task is to make these people interesting or sympathetic to the readers, and that just doesn't happen here.  The murder on the team has the potential for interest, but it's just too hard to get by the other issues to really appreciate it.

title: Alone (The Generations Trilogy)
text:
I'm going to try as best I can to avoid spoilers, but it's hard with this one.

The series was excellent, and most of this book was good until the end. Then to me it just sort of fell apart. Characters started acting strangely. People did things that made no sense for them or for the character. "I really loved X for most of the series, but now I've randomly decided that I will die for Y, even though Y has never reciprocated those feelings". The hero is just swapped out for a new one, and a kind of bland one at that.

The reactions are disproportional to the scope such that some characters seem to have turned into puppets. I expected a lot more of a fight from the characters who were to be sacrificed or who were to have loved ones sacrificed, but they were supposed to be sacrificed, so good little character pawns moved the way the author wanted to move them on the board. Yes, it's war. It's been war since book one. Yes, there have been sacrifices since book one. But being the last book, the stakes are naturally higher, and the resistance that should be there wasn't. And then in the aftermath, the characters who were most impacted, didn't seem to care all that much.

I love Sigler's writing. He's got some amazing books out there, such as the first two books of this series, and to be fair the first 75% or so of this one, but the ending was severely disappointing.

title: Learn to Tat (Book & DVD)
text: Bought this one because it came with a DVD.  What I didn't know was that it was created with a .BUP extension which means I would have to buy additional software for between $40-$70 to play the DVD.  Not very customer friendly.

title: Brave New World
text: I wanted so badly to enjoy this book, and I have wanted to read it for so long, but it was just too dry for my liking. Though, props to Huxley for creating something so timeless as the collection of concepts presented here. The plot of "Brave New World" truly is as relevant today as it was when it was written over 80 years ago. That is a rare talent.

title: The Wand Chooses the Wizard (Harry Potter)
text:
THE WAND CHOOSES THE WIZARD (Harry Potter) is a rather silly interactive book that may amuse a child for a little while. The book is battery-operated, and it comes with a battery-operated plastic wand. (Fortunately, the appropriate disc batteries are included and already installed.) The child needs to be old enough to know NOT to poke someone in the eye with the wand.

When you read through the book, which summarizes the way that Harry Potter used his wand throughout the whole series of Potter books, you come to diagrams of various wand-assisted spells. If you like, you can use the wand to perform the wand motion that accompanies the spell. Then if you press the button on the wand and touch the wand to the diagram in the book, the book plays the spell (e.g., “Accio”) as pronounced by Harry in the Potter movies.

The book isn’t very expensive (about $10.50 on Amazon), but a child will probably tire of it quickly. A child who has read the Potter books won’t need the explanatory text in the book at all. As an adult, I lost interest as soon as I realized that the spell recording would play whenever I touched the wand tip to a spell diagram and pressed the button--there is no need to actually wave the wand in the appropriate pattern to activate the recording.

title: Haiku Knits: 25 Serenely Beautiful Patterns Inspired by Japanese Design
text: This book might appeal to some young people, but there was nothing in it that I would wear.  Not even pretty, in my opinion.  Apart from the cover one, which would please my nieces, nothing for me in there. Sorry.

title: The Fifth Witness (A Lincoln Lawyer Novel Book 4)
text:
I read "The Lincoln Lawyer", and really enjoyed it, so I thought I'd like this one too.
Wrong!
The problem with this book is, .. it's a screenplay, not a "book".
I'm just not into Hollywood pulp.
What a disappointment.

title: Where the Crawdads Sing
text: An absolute tedious and boring book. The details of this character stretch at a pace not fit for reality. Her existence is a dismal portrait of an absolute abandoned child, deserted by all family members, but somehow, amidst the most adverse conditions, she managed to form an existence of sorts. Then, of course, the book gets all these praises with Reese Witherspoon at the lead, which makes it a little intriguing as to how radically it differs from the perspective of some which I subscribe to. No showers/bath for whole seasons, barefooted on such harsh terrain? Survival of the fittest but with one glaring setback: the inconsistencies found along the way, there is a vague resemblance to all things real, difficult to swallow. 2 Stars.

title: Baby Teeth: A Novel
text:
Yes, it is gripping - and, yes, it is well written - but could I find a character that I liked? Sorry, I get turned off by a mother who screams the f-word at her 7 year old child - no matter what that child may be up to. And a father - who is molded to be the perfect husband and father - a supportive, loving, romantic husband, and a piece of jello around his obviously mentally-challenged daughter who is able to manipulate him with ease. And, the daughter, whom any reader will accurately diagnose as deeply troubled and dangerous from the outset. Reading this novel, is akin to waiting to observe a train wreck you know will happen.
The daughter, Hanna, is seven and she does not speak a word, except the occasional “nasty” to her mother - which her father never hears. She must have been exhibiting troubling behavior from a very early age - and it is a bit unbelievable that these parents have allowed this to escalate as long as they have - without the serious intervention of medical professionals. Sending her to school really blows the mind, but these parents try it time after time.
So, as much as the pages turned, the mind turned as well, wondering if the author should have done a bit more research on dangerous child behavior and come up with a more believable set of circumstances and solutions. If this was to be a thriller showcasing parental problems with childhood mental illness, we didn’t need Crohn’s Disease or architecturally green design.
Yes, I felt the ending was setting me up for a sequel - giving Hanna another shot at destroying her mother. I think I’ll sit that one out!

title: Six Years
text: I have read just about all of Coben's books and I am a huge fan of his.  But this stand-alone story of a guy who gets dumped and can't let it go is mediocre  at best.  The story is forced and pro-longed.  It goes on and on trying to rationalize the main character's actions for the reader.  The pill was just too big to swallow.  In addition, the main character kind of seemed unoriginal in that he reminded me of Myron B.  Oh well, still a fan.  Looking forward to the next Myron B. book and hoping it is better than this one.

title: Watercolor with Me in the Jungle
text: This book is half the size of the other books, and it follows the same pattern of an explanation for painting  and a simple drawing. I’m a little disappointed with the series because I feel each book should have a little bit more content and perhaps a new technique so that one could advance from the first book to the third book but that’s not the case. This book is just as simple as the first book if not simpler. The drawings are kind of cartoonish and small for the amount of paper. The subject itself - jungle- could have been explored more with a simple explanation about the plant or animal. Anyway, I used the first two to brush up on my watercolor skills. I don’t know what I’m going to do with this one

title: Trust Me: A Novel
text: This book was like someone threw a basket of fruit in the air and wrote whatever random thought came out. Ashley is accused of killing her daughter . It clearly is based loosely on the Casey Anthony story. Lot of the same plots. With no shame even wrote Casey case into the book.

title: Ivory and Bone
text:
2.5
This book is supposed to be loosely inspired by Pride and Prejudice so if you know that story very well you might see the inspiration. I’m not a huge fan so it was a bit of a stretch for me. That aside, this book was one that you might like if you like ya love stories where the man is pretty much into the girl for a few reason but it seemed a bit to me like the main reason was that there weren’t enough women from their tribe so when they saw a few from another, well, there was some insta-love going on. Of course, this was eons ago so I suppose things were done a bit differently back then.
Seventeen year old Kol lives to hunt and keep his family safe. It’s what he does and what he knows. When Mya, a bold female, arrives from teh south with her family, Kol is smitten. He really wants her to trust him but when he goes to impress her but ends up almost making a deadly mistake, it doesn’t work out so well.
Mya is secretly reeling from a terrible loss after a history with something that happened in the past that Kol isn’t aware of. All of this is brought to light though when another clan arrives. With this clan comes Lo, someone Mya swears has ulterior motives.
As Kol grows closer to Lo, a tension builds up between Mya and Lo that has nowhere to go but into violence. When one thing leads to another, Kol finds he has to question everything he thought he knew.
Although this book is well written, I found myself drifting out of it quite often to the point where I was losing track of characters, clans, and just what was going on at a given point. I don’t even think it’s that hard to follow, I just think it was something about me and this story.
I can’t recommend or not recommend this book. I’ll do it this way. It has a lot of good reviews so I’ll definitely recommend that you go and have a look at the reviews from the others that probably really enjoyed the story. They will likely give you much more insight into what I’m unable to give you insight into. Also, if the book sounds good to you, read it. That’s truly the only way you’ll know if it’s a book for you. Me? It just wasn’t the book for me.

title: I Am Watching You
text: Ugh!  There's 8 hours I'll never get back.  Stupid book!

title: No Way Back: A Novel
text:
Wendy Gould has made a mistake that will haunt her for the rest of her life. Essentially making a decision that will have far reaching consequences and send her running for her life. Following an argument with her husband of 10 years, Wendy goes to meet a friend for a drink when she is tempted to cheat with a reporter/musician. Things go from bad to worse when she witnesses his murder. Confronted by the murderers, she kills one in self-defense and goes on the run from the other.
