She soon learns that everything is not what it seems when she learns of the killer's identities. Wendy must use everything at her disposal to determine who is trying to set her up and why. After all, she has tons to lose, beginning with her freedom if she can't prove her innocence.  

Meanwhile, Lauritzia Velez, thinks that she has escaped her past, and is enjoying her new life as a student and nanny. But when someone tries to kill her while she is at the mall with two kids, she knows that her past has come to haunt her.  She will have to depend on the help of others and her wits to stay alive. 

What I didn't necessarily like about this story.....there is an awful lot of stuff going on in the story....there are Mexican drug lords; a ruthless Cartel leader "El Pirate" (Eduardo Cano); crooked DEA/HSA/FBI agents; illegal gun sales; some gang stuff (Los Zetas); the murders of 3 college students and 2 agents (that seem to be the catalyst of all this); and various other government coverups and conspiracies. I hate to admit it, but that is what I didn't enjoy about this book. There was a little too much going on and at times it became confusing. I also thought that Gross could have done a better job tying the plots together, to make it a more enjoyable read. It seemed to be all over the place at one point. There was foreshadowing and hints of things to come, but some of them never did. I also didn't even care, one way or another, about the main character Wendy. And I know for a fact that if I am ambivalent about the main character, I usually don't invest in the story. In conclusion, I really wanted to like this story, but just couldn't. It was a little disjointed for me, and some of the stuff that happened was unrealistic and illogical. I was very disappointed in "No Way Back".

title: The Oracle Glass
text: although the,story was enjoyable, characters were difficult to follow. I am not a quitter so I finished the book but would not recommend to a friend.

title: The Winter Sea (The Scottish series, 1)
text:
good writing. but i was expecting more historical
fiction and less romance novel

title: A Stranger in the House: A Novel
text: Her first book made me want her second. I started this over a year ago and couldn’t get into it. I forced myself to finish it this evening and half the book was wasted with sitting in a jail cell or the stupid neighbor. The ending sucked. I already bought the next two books before I finished this because o hoped it would’ve been as good as the first. This wasn’t. Many times I keep good books irlve read. This one will be donated to goodwill this week. Save your money and time.

title: Where the Crawdads Sing
text: Started out wonderfully  - very lyrical and compelling - but then the writing deteriorated and the plot got irritatingly melodramatic. I skipped a lot of the last 3/4 of the book - suspend reality if you read this - a 6-year-old on her own - never gets sick, fends entirely on her own until adulthood - hmm I think not

title: Takedown Twenty (Stephanie Plum)
text: Same Old, Same Old.  I realize this is not Pulitzer type material and it's just for fun, but Evanovich owes more to her fans than this. I have eagerly awaited each installment, yet each book gets shorter and shorter. However, the books are expensive for what we are paying for.  It's the same with the audios.  Other authors have up to twenty hours and cost less than Evanovich's paltry six hours. If she isn't going to put forth more effort than this, for which she is being paid handsomely, then she should do what others have suggested and wrap up the series. I hope she reads her reviews and will instead make future books in this series as good as they used to be.

title: Baking: From My Home to Yours
text: I had high hopes for this book, but have been a little disappointed with the recipes. I like that there is a lot of variety (SO MANY RECIPES), which provide great inspiration. However, the recipes I have tried have yielded only mediocre results (not because the recipe didn't work, but because the recipe was not as delicious as I would have hoped). Maybe I need to keep trying to find the diamonds in the rough? I would love to hear from others who have found recipes they love in this book. I haven't given up on it yet, but with so many other cookbooks out there I wonder if it's worth continuing to flip through and hope for the best.

title: Don't Worry, Be Happy Coloring Book Treasury: Color Your Way To A Calm, Positive Mood (Design Originals) 96 Cheerful One-Side-Only Designs on Extra-Thick Perforated Paper in a Spiral Lay-Flat Binding
text: I was a bit disappointed with this book when I received it. I have another from the Author that I really like so I thought.. WOW a spiral bound book with even more of her drawing .. fantastic.. however it seemed like these drawings weren't nearly as well thought out or details as her smaller books are. I was going to return it but after postage and all it just wasn't worth the trouble.  I will pass it along to someone else who might enjoy it as a gift. I'd advise other books of hers if you are looking for the sophisticated adult coloring book type of fun.

title: Valley of the Moon: A Novel
text:
VALLEY OF THE MOON: A NOVEL is a reasonably well-written literary effort, but I don't understand it at all. The plot is basically science fiction, but it's all about love and relationships.

In the novel, a single mother (Lux) of a mixed-race son (Benno) goes on a solo camping trip in Sonoma County's Valley of the Moon (VOTM) in the year 1975. On that trip, she stumbles on a fog-shrouded breach in the very fabric of time.

The breach, created by the 1906 San Francisco earthquake, allows her to pass back and forth from the present day to the distant past--where a farm community that was located at her campsite in 1906 is still operating. The farm inhabitants are trapped by encircling fog (the breach is open in only one direction), but they always welcome Lux warmly on her recurring visits. Time flows at different rates in the past and present-day locales, so that Lux needs to sort out events at both ends of her passages through the breach.

Why this book was written eludes me. I think that my baffled state is caused by the author's very poor handling of the time-and-place transitions in the writing. The story is told in alternating chapters from the viewpoints of two main characters (1906 Joseph) and (1975ff. Lux). However, the narrations of both characters are frequently interrupted with flashbacks to past events in the characters' widely disparate lives in non-VOTM locales. The reader needs the chapter name-and-time labels to even begin to keep track of the novel's overall timeline, and, the reader's job is made even more difficult by the fact that the flashbacks within chapters are indicated only with white-space breaks in the text.

There are a lot of important subsidiary characters (e.g., Rhonda, Beth, Martha, Fancy) that are introduced within each main character's narration by name-dropping--well in advance of the reader's discovering that the character is a significant one. Frequently I had to go back and search for introductions, to find out what the secondary characters' relationships were to the main characters.

Except for those flaws, the writing (e.g., the general prose, the dialogue, the descriptive passages) is pretty good. I was interested in the book because of the VOTM setting--the Jack London State Park area, which is close to where I live, is indeed magical and romantic. However, the lack of a real plot was disappointing, so I rate the book at 3 stars ("It's okay" on the official Amazon scale).

title: Trains and Lovers: A Novel
text:
I'm a huge fan of Alexander McCall Smith's Mma Ramotswe series, but haven't really cared for any of his other series, such as Isabel Dalhausie or the 44 Scotland St ones, because they just don't have the same emotional impact for me personally - they feel a little detached and wishy-washy, as though the story doesn't go anywhere and I don't care enough about the characters to follow them through a meandering tale.

This book was a little more like Isabel Dalhausie and 44 Scotland St in the sense that the characters didn't immediately grab me as being likable and interesting. While I don't mind a slow pace if there is a great deal of charm to the story, this book was right on the edge in terms of holding my interest.

I did finish the book and enjoyed my time with it, but I'm not sure that I'd go so far as to recommend it. It was a perfectly fine, pleasant book, but there was nothing terrifically memorable about either the characters or what happened to them, so it was more a nice way of passing the time than it was a truly special read.

Overall, I wish the author would go back to a style of writing more like Mma Ramotswe and less like Corduroy Mansions or 44 Scotland St. I think there is something in this serialized style which encourages a sense of detachment in both the author and reader.

title: The Lonely Mailman (Whispers in the Forest)
text: The book is dark, darker than is comfortable for a toddler or youngster to appreciate.  Dark and dreamy, but it was hard for him to concentrate.  What was funny was that he really liked the feel of the stone paper which is tree, water and bleach free.  As far as the story is concerned,l it was not easy for him to follow because the mailman's letters and the narration did not flow naturally and I had to point out the format is different for each.  The box was a letter.  One thing I noticed was that the weather vane was labeled as NSEO and if the child speaks English, they may become confused with the O for Oeste which is West in Spanish.  With older children, the possibility of a discussion about when it's okay to write something and sign another's name is okay.  Bottom line of this story is that we call can get along.

title: The Story of Neil Armstrong: A Biography Book for New Readers (The Story Of: A Biography Series for New Readers)
text:
A very simple yet interesting children’s biography of Neil Armstrong. It would be perfect for a child is not a strong reader. Informative with maps, illustrations, and timelines to keep the pages from being just words.
Recommended for any child interested in space, history, or astronauts.

title: Nomad: Designing a Home for Escape and Adventure
text:
I thought this was going to be a guide to designing an escape vehicle via van or rv. I mean, that's exactly what the title implies. It is not that. This is a beautifully produced book with lots of photos of various folks in their mobile homes. It talks a lot about who they are, why they have chosen to go nomadic, and a look at their vehicles of choice.

What it does not do is explain how to get started, what efforts are required to actually live in a hygienic and comfortable manner if you're building out a vehicle or renovating an old rv. This is sort of a glamorous look at the finished product without any behind the scenes info. Sort of like those instagram jokes who never admit that van life means no real toilets and using public showers. As a fluff piece, this is a nice coffee table book.

title: 365 Slow Cooker Suppers
text: Very average recipes, nothing that wasn't available for free online.

title: Absolution (The Penton Vampire Legacy)
text:
The vampire world is in crisis. Seems a vaccination designed to address an epidemic made vaccinated human blood toxic to the blood suckers. Many vampires, like the evil Matthais, are going hungry, while the good vampires aligned under Aidan have entered in to cooperative arrangements with willing humans. Matthais hates Aidan because he is a threat to his power and also because Matthais'son Will is aligned with him. So Mathais kidnaps Mirren, one of Aidan's top lieutenants and a reformed killer. Matthais also captures the lovely Glory,an unvaccinated woman with telekinetic powers. When Will helps Mirren and glory escape,Mathais decides to destory Aidan and his followers once and for all. Meanwhile, Glory learns to control her powers and she and Mirren succumb to their growing attraction. It all ends rather abruptly with the promise of a third book.

I can't say I wa terribly impressed by this book. Granted, I did not read the first book but these sappy good vampire sagas make me long for the days when vampires were souless, demonic monsters. The bad vampires seemed more like the coutry's current crop of politicians, nasty little back-stabbing nits. Old school vampires were darkly seductive,purely evil death machines. The old school victims would not volunteer to be livestock, no matter how generous their tenders. The romance between Glory and Mirren was unconvincing. It was of the "she's hot, he's hot, let's do it" variety instead of th magnetic draw between uwholesome undead and innocent life. The action scenes were pretty good but overall the story and the characters failed to entrance.

title: The Kept Woman: A Will Trent Thriller (Will Trent, 8)
text:
If you are a fan of Slaughter's Will Trent series, as I am, this is an okay read. I would not recommend it to readers new to the story line. There is just too much background information. Indeed, the mystery takes a back seat to the romantic drama between Will, ME Sara and Will's wife, Angie. Will and Angie share a troubled past of abuse and the foster system. While Will aspires to normalcy and believes he has found that in Sara, Angie revels in the dark side. The real mystery is whether Angie has finally gone to far, finally alienating Will forever. The story revolves around unsavory people killing each other. A retired cop known more for his excesses and corruption is found dead in an abandoned construction site. Forensics suggest that a female was severely injured and all that evidence points to the female being Angie. But angie plays all the angles. Playing a part is a basketball player who beat rape charges in a case Will investigated. With Angie playing puppet master, the cops including Will's partner Faith and his boss Amanda, are scrambling to unravel the web of deception and corruption. Sara wonders where Will's heart really lies Will knows he has a choice to make.

I found this book a slow go. The first half drags but it picks up at the end. Angie is probably the liveliest of the bunch this outing but she is hardly sympathetic. Sara seems even more isolated and aloof and Will is just a wimp. Amanda and Faith are just bit players here. Frankly, the fact that will, and his whole team had such a connection with Angie and the basketball player, it seems that none of them should have been involved in the case at all. The scene at the agent's office with Amanda and Will strained belief. The mystery was not riveting especially since the victims were not likable. I prefer a ratio of considerably more suspense than soap opera. This book felt like it was simply setting up situations for the next novel. I usually read this author's books in a single sitting but this one took me the better part of a week. It was less about growth than Will finally seeing the obvious. Not her best but for trent fans, worth a read.

title: Vegan for Everybody: Foolproof Plant-Based Recipes for Breakfast, Lunch, Dinner, and In-Between
text: I bought this as a Christmas gift for a friend and hadn't bothered to open the package until I was ready to gift wrap it.  I opened the book to look through it and found that the cover was on upside down.  Since all the Test Kitchen books are great and it's too late to get a new one, I'm just going to give it as planned and pretend I didn't notice

title: The Night Circus
text:
This book is a definite trip into a strange and peculiar world of pure imagination!
The Night Circus is a one of a kind book. It is written with such brilliant imagery you'll likely remember it long after you've closed the last page. The characters are mostly well written, especially the two main, and the descriptive writing style fills the pages from the very first few sentences to the very last. There are a lot of great ideas in this story and the ending is very unique and well thought out. I don't want to give the story away and with over 6000 reviews already, I'll keep this one relatively short.
The only things that didn't work so well for me that may work perfectly well for so many others were the lack of character depth for a handful of side/main characters and the flow of the story. It wasn't the jumping dates so much as just a disconnect. Other then that the story itself was really very good.
I would definitely recommend this book to anyone interested in the way the synopsis describes the book. If that catches your interest after reading it, then it's very likely the book for you. I enjoyed reading it.

title: Pyrography Basics: Techniques and Exercises for Beginners (Design Originals) Patterns for Woodburning with Skill-Building Step-by-Step Instructions and Advice from Lora Irish on Texture and Layering
text: NIce book but doesn't really cover what I needed. Very, very basic, but nice illustrations. Just doesn't go into enough depth for me. Might be suitable for someone who is thinking about taking up this hobby. I would probably rather have a book that is more A to Z.

title: Skinnytaste One and Done: 140 No-Fuss Dinners for Your Instant Pot®, Slow Cooker, Air Fryer, Sheet Pan, Skillet, Dutch Oven, and More: A Cookbook
text: First of all, I absolutely love Gina's other cookbooks.  In fact, I prepare from these books at least once a week.  So I was thrilled to pre-order this new cookbook.  I have the appliances she targets in this book and was disappointed to find so limited a variety of recipes.  Soup, and more soup.  I watch my calories and carbs.  These recipes are higher in calories and carbs than her previous collections of recipes.  On the bright side, there are several treasures in the book.  the Caldo de Papa (Potato and Short Rib Soup) is deliciously different.  The Italian Stuffed Cubanelle Peppers are awesome but these peppers are not readily available in most grocery stores.  Overall I am not impressed with this cookbook.  I am keeping it because my family has benefited so much from Gina's past cookbooks, her website and blog.  I will try more of these recipes and update my rating if warranted.

title: Down and Out in Beverly Heels
text:
If only the whole book would have lived up to its clever title! "Down and Out in Beverly Heels" tells the story of a once successful, well-to-do actress who now has to pre-audition for roles and has lost everything thanks to her presumed dead con-man husband. The title is cute and relates to Meg's new circumstances. The book has several laugh out loud moments and the Holly wood setting is fun. The author, as an actress who appeared on "Dark Shadows" knows the business and it shows in the realistic details about life as an actress.

Unfortunately, the book is often the confusing. The plot takes several twists and turns which should make the book more interesting, but instead makes the story hard to follow. Meg also reminisces a lot and it's often hard to tell her memories from new events unfolding in the story. Meg is sometimes hard to relate to. She says she is desperate, but then she turns her nose up or complains about legitimate ways she could help get her career back on track. Is it really that horrible to sign autographed photos for fans, who are paying for the privilege of meeting you and getting that picture?! I really like Donna, Meg's fellow Meals on Wheels volunteer, who proves she kinder and shrewder than Meg thought. The scenes with Meg and Donna are some of the best in the book. I like the book's upbeat conclusion, even though some loose ends are left hanging. This wasn't bad for a light read, but didn't live up to the potential of an actress looking for work and for answers about her husband's disappearance.

title: 10-Day Green Smoothie Cleanse
text: Good start but nothing exceptional  that can't be found online, found weekly recipes to be helpful.  Have not started the cleanse, too many events so will  revisit in January 2016, plan to begin Jan 1st.  Bet  Chapel Hill, NC

title: The Shack: Where Tragedy Confronts Eternity
text:
Read the book and found the main character so sad. How could he ever get over not being there for his little girl.

What he went through at the Shack was a monumental time in his life and created a man who was able to forgive the person who murdered his child and also to forgive himself. That's really the only way he could go on living. I felt that he was truly given a gift!

Even though the book was fiction it left me with a feeling of joy that he was able to go on save his marrage and raise the rest of his children with Love.

title: The Fire Thief (A Dark Paradise Mystery)
text:
"The Fire Thief" by Debra Bokur is a mixed bag for me. There are reasons why I wanted to like it. The setting and the ethnic make up of the lead character are something that I identify with. The mystery starts simple enough in that a young surfer is found dead on Maui. The police captain decides to bring in his niece, a detective, onto the case because it looks more suspicious than an accidental death. Kali Mahoe is more than just a detective, she's from a line of kahu and is expected to take the place of her grandmother. So that's another reason why she's brought in.

There are details that I like in the story. The family connections are probably what I feel is best portrayed. The mystery is fairly interesting. The pace is slow and the transitions are a little awkward. I don't know how I feel about the dialogue. Local Hawaii people have a cadence to the way they speak. Even if people in pockets around the islands might sound more like they are from California (hello Lanikai), other places (especially Hilo) still have that local cadence and I don't see any of that in the flat dialogue. At the same time, I am kind of grateful that the writer didn't try to do the local dialect because how some info about the islands and its culture is interspersed through the story was awkward.

Anyway, I do appreciate that a Native Hawaiian heroine was featured and I especially like the character of Walter Alaka'i. The story has some good points too. Yet, overall, the slow pacing and the awkward detailing landed the story in the middle range for me.

title: One + One: Scarves, Shawls & Shrugs: 25+ Projects from Just Two Skeins
text: The book and the designs are excellent. The instructions are wrong, I have to buy the pattern in the german version to make a shawl. Specifically the Stacking Shells Shawl.

title: Out of Order: Stories from the History of the Supreme Court
text: Sandra Day O'Connor is a brilliant and history-making person. Unfortunately I can't say the same for this book. It's not terrible, but I was hoping for more.

title: The Guest List: A Reese's Book Club Pick
text: This book was my book club pick of the month, but can't say if I would have chosen it without the book club.  I guess I'm sorta tired of books where a main character didn't get everything they wanted as a child, and because of that they grow up to be self centered/involved.  It was a so-so book for me.

title: From Bruges with Love (The Pieter Van In Mysteries)
text:
I loved it but two things took away the pleasure - it needed editing for American readers - too many main characters names beginning with the letter V, compounded with their being too many characters. The 2nd was the gratuitous graphic violence mostly toward women.

What I did like was that it is a police procedural, with more twits and surprises than a reader could hope for and they all made complete sense. I also loved the last chapter and the shoot out - like Dirty Harry meets Die Hard - loved it.

Translated and originally published in 1997 featuring Deputy Commissioner Van In, his openly gay Sgt. Guido Versavel, his BFF forensics expert Leo Vanmaele, and his lover Assistant District Attorney Hannelore Maartens.

BOOK #1 The Square of Revenge: An ancient family name hides sinister secrets of love and betrayal, riches and revenge in the heart-pounding English-language debut by European crime sensation Pieter Aspe. The beautiful medieval architecture of Bruges belies the dark longings of her residents. When the wealthy and powerful Ludovic Degroof’s jewelry store is robbed, nothing is stolen, but the jewels have been dissolved in jars of aqua regia, an acid so strong that it can melt even gold. In the empty safe is a scrap of paper on which a strange square has been drawn. At first, Inspector Van In pays little attention to the paper, focusing on the bizarre nature of the burglary. But when Degroof’s children begin to receive letters with this same enigmatic square, Van In and the beautiful new District Attorney, Hannelore Martens, find themselves engaged in solving the mystery of a complex web of Latin phrases, a baroness’ fallen family, and Degroof’s unsettling relationship with a hostage grandchild, who is being ransomed for a priceless collection of art.
BOOK #2 ‘The Midas Murders’ Inspector Van In races against the clock to thwart a series of terrorist plots. One quiet snow-covered Sunday morning in Bruges, a prominent business executive is found dead in the streets, apparently due to an alcoholic hemorrhage, but for Inspector Van In, there is a something about the autopsy that does not add up. When he questions the businessman’s friend, a Dutchman, he too is found dead the next morning, burned to death in a house fire. When there is an explosion in the middle of a popular tourist area in downtown Bruges, Van In strives to find the connection between the three incidents, but no one is coming forward to claim responsibility for this terrorist attack. Just an anonymous letter to the police, threatening more bombings'unless they cooperate with a series of demands that would undermine the entire city government. Aided by the spunky and beautiful assistant DA Hannelore Maartens, Inspector Van In finds himself enmeshed in the case that threatens not just the lives of countless of innocent people, but the heart of the city he loves

title: The Harvest Man (Scotland Yard's Murder Squad)
text:
THE HARVEST MAN is the fourth book in the "Scotland Yard's Murder Squad" series, which begins with [[ASIN:0425261271 The Yard]]. It seems to be a close continuation of the previous series book, which featured Jack the Ripper ([[ASIN:0399166432 The Devil's Workshop]]). This fourth book doesn't work well as a standalone, because a new reader (1) doesn't at all understand the hold that "Saucy Jack" has over Inspector Walter Day; and (2) doesn't know anything about what put the "Harvest Man" into a lunatic asylum or how he escaped from the asylum. (Given the cliffhanger at the end, the fifth book in this series probably won't work as a standalone, either.)

The Harvest Man--who hides in unsuspecting victims' attics (like the harvest spider he is named for)--is murdering married couples, after first carving up their faces. Someone else (could it be the escaped "Jack the Ripper"?) is killing and removing the internal organs of local "working women". The first investigation is being handled by Inspector Tiffany, who reluctantly consults with the deskbound Inspector Day, at Dr. Kingsley's insistence. The second investigation comes to light through former Sergeant Hammersmith's private efforts toward collaring Jack, and also involves Dr. Kingsley and Inspector Day. Most of the plot events center around the activities of Inspector Day's family and friends, and around the activities of two boys and a young wife who have seen, and narrowly escaped from, the Harvest Man.

The book is set in London in the Spring of 1890, though it's somewhat lacking in the historical detail that would firmly anchor it there. In fact, the leisurely murder investigations seemed curiously modern to me. Although the investigators are limited to communicating in person or by messenger, I kept wanting them to pull out their cell phones and get on with it! As previously mentioned, the characters' qualities, backgrounds, and motivations seem to have been developed in earlier series books. I liked Claire, Fiona, Henry, the doctor, and the detectives, but I know little about them beyond their activities in this book. The one really good character is the deaf shopkeeper who insists to everyone that "you don't need to shout". The Harvest Man himself is totally one-dimensional--just a lunatic driven by a single mad obsession that forces him to carve up and remove his victims' faces.

Some reviewers have complained that the crimes are described with too much grisly detail. It's true that the crime scenes are bloody messes, but none of the victims are tortured. The reader isn't subjected to graphic descriptions of a victim's pain or of a sadistic, psychopathic killer's pleasure in inflicting it. For me, grue (lots of blood) is to be expected in a mystery/thriller novel, and it doesn't bother me.

But in my opinion, THE HARVEST MAN isn't worth the time it takes to read it, if you're new to the series. The basic face-carver plot is too slender to justify a full-length novel, and you need to be familiar with the good-person characters already if you're going to care much about what happens to them. Also, I was baffled by the inclusion of several poems from Robert Louis Stevenson's [[ASIN:1402750625 A Child's Garden of Verses]]--were these intended to enhance the historical atmosphere? For these reasons, I rate this mystery/thriller at 3 stars ("It's okay" on the official Amazon scale).

title: In the Company of Others (The Mitford Years)
text: I have read many Jan Karon/Mitford novels and have loved each and every one.  This one, however, has been my least favorite of them all.  I began to wonder if Karon had actually written this one.  It was not up to her masterful style of writing.  The Irish dialect and especially the Journal "dissertations"  bogged down what otherwise would have been relaxing reading, as are her other books.  The Journal entries could have been shortened considerably; acutally, I skip read a lot of them and I still maintained the story line.  I don't like to skip read, but the Journal entries became boring.

title: The Horse Healer: A Novel
text:
This book is about the history of Spain between 1195 and 1212. If you're intrigued primarily by the "horse healer history" aspect, I'll warn you that it's only a small part of the story. Diego doesn't even met the horse healer until page 42. In the first 250 pages of the book, only about 17 pages described horse healing activities. The story is about politics, religion, science, rape, sex, seduction, violence, and death. The author has clearly done a lot of research into the time period, but it wasn't the story I was expecting.

Diego's (Christian) family is killed or captured by certain Muslim warriors. He's periodically determined to find his two sisters that have been captured. The story splits, and we also follow the lives of the girls who are: raped, raped, raped, one's beheaded, raped, whipped, etc. In Diego's storyline, women keep trying to seduce and have sex with him. For example, his horse healing lessons involve learning Arabic, but all he's thinking about is how seductive and sensuous his master's wife is.

The story is more than just sex and violence, but that sure seemed to make up most of it. Luckily, the sex/rape scenes weren't very long or very graphic, and the gore wasn't generally described even if the violence was. There was a minor amount of bad language. The kind people all seem to have the philosophy of "there are many ways to God" while a friar is one of the crueler characters in the story. Diego is willing to suffer that friar's cruelty for the chance to read ancient books on science and mystical thought. Yup, he's sisters are a big priory to him...when he can bother to think about them.

The original story arc setup was Diego trying to save his sisters out of his guilt for not preventing their capture, but then he'd get so focused on his professional ambitions that he'd apparently go for years without even thinking about them. It's like the author got confused about his intended storyline: Diego saving his sisters or Diego making a name for himself as a horse healer. It could have included both successfully, but the way this was written just didn't work for me.

title: Trance (Alex Madison Book 1)
text: I found the gore excessive. The plot was a bit confusing and too fantastic.

title: Last Day
text: Not sure I will read any more of the free novels after this one.

title: The Best American Nonrequired Reading 2014
text:
This is such a strange book. I'm really rating it as more of a 2.5/5 rounded up to 3 stars. First of all, I didn't do enough research on this book as it wasn't what I expected. I thought it was going to be a book of lists, but it actually contains the writings, although there is a short list at the back of the book that contains more stories that didn't make it into the book.

I enjoyed the introduction by Lemony Snicket, it was amusing, but most of the book fell flat for me. There were some stories that I enjoyed, but most of them were just really weird or boring. It was a little bit of a chore to get through this book. I had to give myself a reward to convince myself to pick this book up (read one excerpt, then you can read another book). This is definitely the type of book that you read slowly--a few stories at a time.

It would have been nice to see some sort of introduction before the stories explaining why they were chosen or why someone thought they were important.

title: A Spool of Blue Thread
text: This was certainly not my favorite Anne Tyler book.  The first part was plodding and not particularly interesting.  None of the characters grabbed my attention and I felt no affinity with any of them.  The second part, which was a historical reference, was more interesting but still nothing special.  Having said that, I should have stopped reading it and moved on to something more compelling.  When I get a book I really enjoy, I cannot wait to finish it, but also dread finishing it because I will get no further enjoyment out of such a book.  This book is definitely NOT one of those.  It took forever to read and I couldn't wait to finish it so I could read something better.

title: House Immortal
text:
This is not a bad book, but it suffered from me also having read a much better version of the same story by Tanith Lee: Metallic Love. And I am sorry, but Tanith Lee tells the story of a person waking in a robotic body so much better. This was a sometimes uneven read for me and it seems like the story might have run away a bit. It is a romance, not a science fiction and that was my main problem with it. If I am reading a ROC science fiction , I want to read an honest science fiction, not a dewey eyed girl interested in the perfect physique of her male companion.

There are elements of classic Science Fiction in it. (I got a bit of Piers Anthony's Phase/Photon series in the house colors and their areas of expertise, Some Edgar Rice Burroughs Princess of Mars, There is also a Centurian aspect or Forever Soldier in this, along with a bit of several other pulp classics) but in the end, it is entertaining, but not mind blowing. Will I pick up the next in the series? Maybe, if I am in the mood for more romance, but probably not if I want a science fiction story.

title: Watercolor with Me in the Jungle
text: I love these books but this one seems thinner than the last two.

title: After the Crash: A Novel
text:
I thought the premise of this book was fascinating. A plane crash in the Alps leaves one survivor- a baby girl. No one knows who this baby really is- she could be either of the two baby girls who were on board. Both families try to lay claim to the girl, and a private detective spends years trying to find out the truth. Along the way there are many, many complicating factors. The detective discovers a dramatic secret about the case, but he is murdered before he can reveal it. At the end we find out the truth.

While this all sounds enticing there were problems with this book. The writing can get very ponderous at times. This is not a book I read fervently from beginning to end- I kept putting it aside. More annoying, the ending relies on a deus ex machina, which I hate in a mystery. I want to try and solve the mystery myself. So, while this was not the worst book I've read this year, it did have some distinct problems, and I think there are plenty of other, better choices.

title: Dexter's Final Cut: A Novel (Dexter Series)
text: While I loved the early Dexter novels, I was sorely disappointed in this one.  Dexter and his sister Deb are acting as consultants for a CSI-like TV show. Dexter develops a school boy crush on the female lead, a stereotypical  actress, blonde and beautiful. And therein lies the biggest problem. Dexter loses his edge. The rapier wit is exchanged for a plastic utensil and the dark subversive anti-hero has been swapped for a sap.  Rita and the step brats are beyond irritating and the ending is preposterous. It is a sorry and unsatisfying end to one of the most entertaining and original series. Pass.

title: Seeing Red
text: Better than some, not as good as others. Author's as prolific as Brown can't always hit home runs.  I suspect readers new to Brown will enjoy the book.  Brown's long time readers (since 1980's) will be unimpressed.  Plot uninspired, no surprises. One dimensional characters. Simply not what we expect from Brown.

title: 500 Low-Carb Recipes: 500 Recipes, from Snacks to Dessert, That the Whole Family Will Love
text: This style of diet is not for me.  I gave the book away.

title: Far from Fair
text:
I was drawn to this book just because of the traveling done in an RV part of the story. I go through cravings for stories like those, and they're so hard to find! (Feel free to give me more suggestions!) :)

This book ended up having so much packed into it, but was still a slower type of read. I enjoyed the writing style, though! I enjoyed the family and support in Arnold's last book, too, but also had a few issues with it, just like this one.

Let's look at a few things I didn't like.

Odette's attitude stinks! It's pretty accurate for *most* children her age, though there are plenty of lovely exceptions. I was there once, and I still remember my stinky attitude. I sure didn't need to read books with more stinky attitudes and think that it's okay to be that way.

While traveling in the RV, Odette hears her parents doing things parents do together. Really?! Is this necessary in a children's book? I know children that age know about "the birds and the bees", but I don't think they need to be reading books that make them visualize their parents in bed together. I simply thought this was a ridiculous addition to the book. It also seemed out of place, since their parents are struggling so bad. I won't go into great detail, because it's somewhat of a spoiler, but the parents are so wishy-washy in this book that I can't imagine how the children can cope with it. THAT might be the reason for Odette's stinky attitude!

Also, the story doesn't have an ending. Why is this happening more and more with books? It drives me insane! You can decide for yourself what the family decides to do, I suppose, but when I'm reading a book, I want to ending provided for me, generally.

SPOILER AHEAD!!!!!!!!!
I didn't necessarily dislike this addition to the story, but I can't say I liked it either. Odette's grandmother has cancer, and she has decided to "die with dignity"(though I don't recall if those exact words were used in the book). Odette and her friend talk about their concerns, but this form of dying is given neither a positive or negative image within the book. It didn't feel like the author was trying to voice her opinion, which I greatly appreciate. She was simply writing about a tough topic that is happening in the world these days.

END SPOILER

Another topic tackled is autism. Odette's brother has autism. I don't recall this ever being said straight out in the book, but it is mentioned in the description. At first, I didn't realize anything was specifically different about Odette's brother, but more than halfway through, I realized. I then turned to the back and read the description(which I typically avoid until after reading a book).

Throw in a boy crush(diversity added here, which is great), and the ups and downs of friendship, and there's a ton of stuff happening. Again, though, it's a slower type of read, even if I quickly made my way through it AND enjoyed it, despite my many dislikes.

In the end, I don't really think that this will be a book for most kids the target age. For those that enjoy somewhat sad books FULL of everyday struggles, and then some, this might be the perfect book for them, minus the part of the parents in their bed.

title: The Cottage at Glass Beach: A Novel
text:
Nora Keane has a troubled past and a troubled present. Her mother disappeared when Nora was five, and her marriage has been rocked by an affair. To get away from the scandal of her husband's affair, Nora packs up her two daughters and retreats to the small Maine Island where she was born. At the island Nora tries to sort out her marriage and find out what, exactly, happened to her mother.

The island is deeply steeped in Celtic mythology, and residents believe in the magical qualities of the sea. They believe that the sea brings creatures like selkies to aid mortals, and certainly some of the characters on the island have other-worldly qualities. This book is hardly fantasy, but it deals with how mythology functions in people's lives. These beliefs are particularly salient on a small island reliant on a tempestuous ocean for its safety and its livelihood.

Though Nora has clearly suffered, she is not always an especially likable character. She doesn't seem to take her older daughter's feelings about her home and father very seriously. I also found the resolution of the final series of events to be entirely unbelievable. In sum, not a bad book, and definitely well-suited to summer reading.

title: Sidney Sheldon's The Tides of Memory
text:
I think I am the only reader that believes when a book is co-authored...something is lost. This book is more Tilly Bagshawe than Sidney Sheldon. Yes, there is a plot with twists and turns with surprises along the way and at the end. I really enjoyed the beginning of the book, when the Toni and Billy spend a summer at camp and the tragic outcome. However, once the story advances to their adulthood, I started to lose interest. Perhaps, it was the jet-set self-centered characters (except the husbands of Lucy and Alexia)and the total disregard of consequences.

This is a fast, roller coaster read to escape by.

title: A Prisoner of Birth: A Novel
text:
I was not sure if this book should receive 3 o r 4 stars. I wanted to give it 3 1/2 so I settled on 3
As usual Archer penned a great story,plot was good and held my interest to the last page.
My only complaint is that there could have been less details about some of the characters,as it felt a bit like rambling.

title: The Boundless
text:
Adventure story of Courage, Honesty, Faithfulness and Growing up. The first transcontinental Canadian railroad (circa 1885), a deadly avalanche and murder.

In three years time 17 year old Will Everett's family has made it rich. Now he on the maiden trip of the new 900 car luxury locomotive 'The Boundless'. Will is an artist, unlike father (who manages the railroad). Will has the key to the railway owner's, Cornelius Van Horne, funeral car, which puts him in extreme danger from criminals who want to raid the car for it's purported wealth.

Aiding Will are:

Dorian, is ringmaster of the 'Zircus Dante'.

Maren, is a tightrope walker (and escape artist!) with the circus.

Mixing in Canadian folklore add to the adventure.

Will has the behavior of a 10 to 13 year that struck me as very odd for someone heading off to college in a year.

title: Shakespeare's Sonnets
text: It's just nice having "pocket sonnets" on my phone.  Hard to find the one I want to read at the moment though.

title: Borrowed Time
text:
This is the 2nd book in a series, which I didn't know. Usually, there's some indication in the product page, but I didn't notice any this time. Maybe it was because I didn't read the first book, but I didn't care for it. I couldn't get into the story, and was left confused.

If you want to read this book, make sure you start with Chronal Engine.

title: My First Library : Boxset of 10 Board Books for Kids
text: The size is perfect for little hands. The examples in the book are off. Farm animals has a camel, Christmas tree for the letter x. He is 1 1/2 so he isn't affected. The team of experts might look at what makes proper examples

title: Eating Vegan: A Plant-Based Cookbook for Beginners
text:
I think pictures are important in a recipe book. I think it is especially important when creating a recipe book for beginners, and doubly so if it is a recipe book for beginners for vegan eating, as that denotes a life style change. Pictures are important to help some see what a finished item should look like. And of course, least of all pictures help whet the appetite.

There are 26 photos all together. 75 recipes. This is not 26 photos of completed recipes.. just photos. Some may present as a recipe, many are just visual fodder. All the pictures are nice enough, but there simply aren't enough to go a long with the wide range of recipes is this book. For those that base their recipe book buying based on the inclusion of helpful pictures, know that you will be disappointed.

I happen to know, and can imagine what a majority of these recipes should look like. I am not a vegan, not even a vegetarian, but I enjoy cook and eating pretty much all food but especially if it is plant based. So over the years I have become familiar with the lingo, the various products and substitutions, and how it should look. Do you know what makes me salivate for a plant based dish? THE PICTURES. I may absolutely prefer a vegan dish over some meat based dish based on how it looks--simply because the photo allowed me to see a meal presented in a super favorable and mouth watering manner. Many years ago I remember losing my mind SEEING a recipe for "steaks" using a Seitan recipe. Had I just read about it? I bet I would have passed it over. Pictures matter.

I think this is especially important when a book is classified for beginners. It tells me that someone is either new to cooking (so please have pics/illustrations) or new to plant based meals (again, help a newbie along by having pics for every recipe, or near every recipe.). If it were JUST a book on vegan recipes, then I may simply say- "if your preference is to have pics, this doesn't have it, but the recipes are varied, interesting, and what I have made is delicious"-- and the lack of pics wouldn't go into a rating. But in this scenario it does, because it very well does a disservice to would-be plant based eaters trying to learn to cook/cook plant based foods.

Now all that being said.. the recipes! 75 RECIPES! This is favorable. There are a variety of recipes for all sorts of tastes and occasions divided into chapters. There are a couple of chapters that I will refer to as a primer on vegan eating and what you want in the kitchen. I think the core of the recipes are amenable to a varied diet with assortment of tastes and preferences.

*******CHAPTERS****
ix: Introduction
1. Chapter 1: Vegan Eating 101
7. Chapter 2: Getting into the Kitchen
23. Chapter 3: Breakfast (10 recipes)
39. Chapter 4: Salads, Soups, Stews (13 recipes)
59. Chapter 5: Snacks and Sides (8 recipes)
71. Chapter 6: Handhelds (11 recipes)
93. Chapter 7: The Main Event (13 recipes)
115. Chapter 8: Something Sweet (8 recipes)
129. Chapter9: Homemade Essentials (12 recipes)
147. Resources
151. References
152. Index

BREAKDOWN/INFO included with each RECIPE:
- how many servings
-prep time
-cook time
-whether is is gluten free, left over friendly, nut free, plan ahead, 30 mins or less
-a short personal intro blurb
-ingredients needed
-steps/directions
-first timer tip
-substitution tip
-nutritional information per serving (cals, carbs, fat, fiber, protein, sodium, iron)

OVERALL, the breakfast and salads section are mostly skippable for me. The recipes of interest for me include anything that is a meat alternative (again, SEITAN!), as well as other hearty dishes like "mac-n-cheese", pizza, casseroles. Deserts are always good, too. Some of my favorite cookies and dessert breads in life happen to be ones made from vegan recipes. The dessert recipes in here are ok. Fun to see a snickerdoodle flavored hummus, but how about a snickerdoodle recipe? (again, I make a mean vegan snickerdoodle). This is a preference things, so while this leaves very few recipes I am interested in overall, I do like the variety that it could give someone else. No rating adjustment for this.

I have made the Avocado Toast. I have been eating smashed avocado on toast for years. I like it on a super dense bread (Food For Life Ezekial- find in freezer section), and thought I would give their take a try by making the exact recipe in this book.It was good, not sure if a recipe is really needed for that. However, it gave me opportunity to try making the cashew cream cheese it called for. I happened to have some cashews that needed to be used up, so I made the cashew Cream cheese (and later, cashew mayo). As a non vegan, these are all ok. I usually just go with actual cream cheese or mayo, but I am all for learning how to make something similar with other items I have on hand.

I haven't made everything, but I have made several. Recipes I would make again and found helpful:
Stuffed Peppers
Sweet and Sour tofu
Kung Pao Seitan
mac and "cheese"
Pasta Carbonara

I especially liked the seitan recipe as it gave directions on how to make it in the oven, something I had not done before. I had been making mine by simmering (which the blurb does mention), but this lent to a greater meat texture. I have made seitan "steaks" with "gravy" in the past, and it always seemed to need a little something different, so in my case it came down to a different technique. YAY! I might try it in the flautas/taquitos recipe, but I may instead opt for a "steak" fajita application. I have a flauta recipe using mushrooms and beans that is already out of this world. Anyway, if you haven't tried seitan, and have no gluten issues, I highly recommend learning how to make it, especially if you want a "meat" taste and texture. The recipe in here is good. I can make seitan for my husband, who would otherwise never intentionally make a "vegan" dish. Also, when at the store, look for Tempeh. It is one of my most favorite foods ever. It is so versatile- from crumbling it up as "ground meat" texture to lightly frying into strips and dunking in some awesome dip/hummus of choice.

Final thoughts on this book: I think the recipes are reasonable-- not too complex for a beginner, straight forward clear information, and the "first timer tips" seems informative and helpful. Even so, I don't love the book, and I wouldn't recommend it to a vegan newb. I have vegan/vegetarian cookbooks that I LOVE, and this doesn't fit that. I am likely to pass this on to someone as I don't think it has a place in my kitchen library. I think the seitan recipe is the best of the bunch. The severe lack of pictures make this book underwhelming. Usually it comes down to: if I were at a physical book store, would I pick this up? would I flip through it? Would I ponder buying it? Would I ultimately buy it or put it back down? I would initially pick this up (the cover is of Buffalo Cauliflower, and who doesn't like that?), I would take a quick peek, but once I saw the lack of pics, I would put it back down with no intention to by. If I happened to see the seitan recipe, I would have jotted down a note about it, but likely would not have bought. Not when there are lots and lots of other vegan/plant based recipe books out there. IF I happened to desperately want certain recipes in my collection, then that would be taken into consideration (looking at you again, Seitan).. but in this case, I still think I would have bypassed this book in search of something else.

So this book is.. "OK". Not bad, not great. Not what I would have in mind as a "beginner" level recipe book, if only for lack of pics.

I know what I don't like may not be bothersome to you, so I hope that at least the information I provided is helping you make a decision today. Take care. Be well!

title: Instant Pot Pressure Cooker Cookbook: 500 Everyday Recipes for Beginners and Advanced Users. Try Easy and Healthy Instant Pot Recipes.
text: Some good recipes unfortunately the settings are generic.

title: New Guide to Coloring for Crafts, Adult Coloring Books, and Other Coloristas!: Tips, Tricks, and Techniques for All Skill Levels! (Design Originals) Step-by-Step Lessons & 100 Ready-to-Color Designs
text: This book is nice, but I feel like it tries to do too much.  It has a nice section on color theory, but some of the info in other parts makes me wonder.  For example, it says that colored pencils are a "fast" way to color a large area in a short amount of time.  Really?  Colored pencils are known to be extremely slow -- small point, require many layers to build up color.  Stuff like that makes me question the rest of the information in the book.  But it would probably be fine for most people looking for a way to improve their coloring skills and color knowledge.  It could also be nice for someone who thinks they might like coloring, but aren't sure.  That's why I bought it.  It has a nice variety of coloring pages in the back.  It turns out that I'm not all that interested in coloring books, and have jumped to drawing/illustration.  I gave this book to my child, who really likes it.

title: Night Film: A Novel
text:
Scott McGrath is a disgraced journalist who lost his credibility after making accusations against the infamous film maker Stanislas Cordova, a man whose movies are so scary the movie studios stopped stopped showing them and they can only be seen underground, shown by his huge cult following. Cordova hasn't been seen in years, his movies were shrouded in secrecy. It isn't certain he is still alive. But when his 24 year old daughter, Ashley Cordova is found dead at the bottom of an elevator shaft in an abandoned building, Scott McGrath decides to investigate, hoping to get to Cordova and restore his good name. But Scott ends up with unexpected company on his journey; Hopper, a former boyfriend of Ashley's and Nora, a coat check girl who saw Ashley before she died.
I read this book over a month ago and I honestly still am not sure how what I think about it. When I first started it I almost chucked it against the wall because I found the overuse of italics to be infuriating. They were so randomly used and I had no idea what the author was trying to convey. I decided to continue reading because I had heard it was so good. I found a big chunk of the novel to be less suspenseful and more comedic. This was in part due to the italics. Again, I don't know if this is what the author intended. But with McGrath, Hopper, and Nora on the trial to recreate Ashley's last days and their encounters with a myriad of unusual people, it is practically a comedy of errors.
Pessl adds a lot to her story via fake newspaper articles, photos and other documents. I found this added rather than detracted from the story so that was a plus. There is a 80-100 page chunk in the last quarter that was so hard to follow and was either horrible or brilliant. I'm still deciding. The ending is more then one ending and there is more than one twist. Does the reader get the truth? Does Scott? This is probably a book that has to be read again to pick up what might have been missed the first time. I just don't think I can do it, I felt drained when I finished. A very unusual novel.

title: The Girl with the Dragon Tattoo: A Lisbeth Salander Novel (The Girl with the Dragon Tattoo Series Book 1)
text: i can't believe this book rates so well. i do not know why. hopefully the movie will be better.

title: Shell Game: A V.I. Warshawski Novel (V.I. Warshawski Novels)
text:
Two investigations push PI Warshawski to her limits. The first involves the nephew of close friend and almost surrogate mother Lotty. The nephew, Felix, an engineering student , is involved in as group that includes Middle Eastern student. He issoon implicated in as murder and suspected of being a terorist due to his assoications. The second involves the disappearance of the niece of Wrshawski's sleaze of an ex-husband. This puts her at odds with rich and powerful players in the corporate world. IN the meantime she is juggling the demands of running her own PI business.

I can't say I was impressed with the twentieth outing of V I Warshwski. It was slow and I never engaged or particuarly cared about any of the characters. Additionally her political rants grew tiresome. No problem with outrage against perceived injustice but spare me the political lectures in formats that are supposed to be entertaining. If you are devoted to the series this may be worth a read. Otherwise look under a different shell.

title: As Chimney Sweepers Come to Dust: A Flavia de Luce Novel
text: I have read all of the previous Flavia books, and found this one to be a little slow. I finished it anyway, and the author found an ending that made me happy!  This little girl is too intelligent for her own good, obstreperous, adventuresome, and always entertaining!

title: Junior Maker: Experiments to Try, Crafts to Create, and Lots to Learn!
text:
JUNIOR MAKER is an “ activity book” for very young children. The target age isn’t given, but the book seems to be intended for young readers (2d or 3d grade?). There are some projects that might be fun (e.g, making a Roman mosaic from colored paper squares, or building a pipe cleaner frame for blowing square bubbles, or making a hanging bird feeder from gelatin and birdseed).

However, the projects are tied to very general topics (space, nature, history, animals, science), and seem too random to be teaching much of anything. Mainly the book seems to be a “keep the child busy” book, perhaps intended for use over summer vacation?

title: Cake Decorating With The Kids: 30 Modern Cakes and Bakes for All the Family to Make
text:
Although this book is chock full of pretty pictures, the directions are spare and insufficient for kids or those with limited experience. Written by two British bakers, it contains terminology which at times both confusing and annoying. Note that in their recipes they use castor (superfine) sugar and "self-raising" flour. Neither confectioners sugar (what they call icing sugar) nor granulated sugar can be substituted. Super fine sugar can be purchased at a premium in most supermarkets but is more easily made by placing granulated sugar in a food processor until it is powdered. Similarly, self rising flour, while a staple in the south, is rare where I am in New York. Again, it can be easily made by combining on cup of all purpose flour less two tsps with 1 1/2 tsp baking powder and 1/2 tsp salt. They suggest you buy your sugarpaste (fondant) and petal paste (gum paste) and do not provide recipes for them. I find homemade preferable to the store bought and neither is terribly difficult to prepare. There is also something referred to as "sugardough" in the Teddy Bear Picnic project. I am not sure what that is, I am assuming fondant. They provide measurements both in weight ( grams, ounces) not volume (cups). The conversions from metric to english are odd, ie, 9 3/4, 11 1/4, 7 ounces. Most little kids get a kickout of using measuring cups, not scales. There is also apparently a difference between European and American eggs. You can use a Euroopean large or an American extra large. Apparently there is no need to substitute large American eggs for European eggs as nothing is mentioned in the recipes where they use medium eggs.

The projects themselves are cute but not wildly inventive. I have seen versions of pretty much all of the designs. I don't find that they are especially directed for kids, although they are charming.

There are only a few recipes. I was going to make the sugar cookies but noticed that there was no baking powder or soda included. This was one of the recipes that actually used all purpose flour not self rising. All the sugar cookie recipes I have made included one or the other. So instead, I made the maple frosting. It was good although I did add a pinch of salt.

Althogh this is an attractive book, it is not one I will be using. Experienced decorators will be uninspired and the novices will be confused. The projects are not especially kid friendly and not broken down into easily manageable steps. Sure you could have the roll some fondant or cut out some decorations but you could do that without this book.

title: The Stories We Tell: A Novel
text:
This is a story about a family on the edge of splintering. Eve and Cooper are the perfect couple with the perfect family in Savannah, or so it seemed from the outside. A mysterious car crash starts events that show the cracks in the perfect family veneer. Eve is unsettled with Cooper's story and starts to search for answers on her own, and the secrets come to the surface. There are twists and turns along the way, but I was not surprised at the ending.

I wanted to love this book because I have enjoyed Patti Callahan Henry's previous books. This book starts strong with a mystery at the beginning but seemed to stall. Unfortunately, I could not connect to the characters or the storyline. I was disappointed because I do look forward to her books.

title: Three to Get Deadly (Stephanie Plum, No. 3) (Stephanie Plum Novels)
text: I liked this story. This is the 3rd one I've read, and it's pretty much the same as the first two. I could see more character development of Lula and Ranger, though Ranger's still a mystery. This book wasn't as funny as the others, though I still laughed (a little bit). Stephanie seems to have learned a little more about being a bounty hunter, but I find her life to be repetitious. The same thing happens over and over again: wake up, shower, feed Rex, do bounty hunting stuff, eat at parents, go home (in a different car EACH TIME), answer door for Morelli who has brought a pizza... and the ending is anti-climactic... they leave out the back door of the cabin and roll down the hill, and next scene Stephanie's getting out the shower. It just cuts out way too quickly for me, almost like the author wanted to wrap things up, or got fed up with it; granted, it was still a good story, not much of an interesting mystery (although I really had no idea what was going on until about halfway through), and it wasn't suspenseful at all. Still no chemistry between Stephanie and either of the guys. And what's up with Stephanie's diet anyway? She eats more than 2 doughnuts every day (after breakfast), cheeseburgers, fries, pizza, fried chicken... and yet she's 5'7 and 125 lbs? Even a person with high metabolism can't escape the effects of such fatty foods eaten so often. And I mean this is every day, not just once in a while. Even still, it was a quick read and the author kept me intrigued. I'm not sure anymore if I'd recommend these, and I'm not sure if I'm gonna continue on with the series. Probably will, since I've taken a liking to the characters, though be it a small liking.

title: Bonita Avenue: A Novel
text:
Like a Greek Tragedy this novel plays out. Translated in 2010 from Dutch to English and first published in Holland. This story is all over the place, literally. It alternates between narrators and country's Belgium, California, the Netherlands, and Shanghai.

Siem Sigerius, a professor of mathematics and judo champion, at one time living on Bonita Avenue in Berkeley, California now chancellor of a university in the Netherlands. Siem see's his oldest step-daughter, Joni, in a porno movie he is watching after he is dumped by his lover, a student, when he will not divorce his wife. Alrighty then. His oldest daughter's boyfriend, Aaron, is like the son he never had; the son he did have is a one that parental nightmares are made of. Believe it or not, there is a lot more, but that would spoil the suspense.

title: All Fall Down (Embassy Row, Book 1): Book One of Embassy Row (1)
text: This was an unsatisfying thriller.  There IS some suspense, but it all revolves around one issue, and there are no clues building up to the big reveal, it's just dropped on you 280-odd pages into the 310-page novel.  Then there's the climactic confrontation, after which we get a character who explains everything to wrap it up.  The author's style is good and the pacing is fairly effective, but I found the plotting disappointing.

title: The Perfect Handmade Bag: Recycle and reuse to make 35 beautiful totes, purses and more
text: Most of these bags are pretty basic. I have made many of them before without a pattern. There was nothing in this book that I want to make right away. Nice ideas for a beginning sewer or bag maker.

title: Emily Post's Manners in a Digital World: Living Well Online
text:
You'd think manners online was something everyone basically just knew. Yet, think about the real world. When was the last time someone opened a door for you, or you opened a door for someone else? Sometimes we forget about manners - both in the real world and the virtual world.

I've been net connected since the early 90's, when most people didn't even own a home computer, and I've been working online for most of that time. Currently, I manage social media accounts for small businesses. Much of the information in this book truly needs to be considered by anyone representing a company online as well as managing personal profiles. I've seen it all, and usually, communication online can turn into disputes, and knowing how to mind your online manners can truly make or break your image. Unlike being rude to a passing stranger in the real world, being rude online means that it STAYS there for anyone to see through all eternity! Your little manner misstep ends up haunting you endlessly!

I would have loved this book a lot more had it contained less of a primer of what each aspect of the Internet was in a nutshell and actually had more information on manners. It's not easy to use as a reference book, and it's hard to sort through all the 'primer' material to get to the info that's relevant. For instance, one of my pet peeves is all the bathroom 'selfies' that people post online. Not just teen girls, either! Yet, I couldn't find a way to research that in the book (the final print may have an index, but the uncorrected proof doesn't). Also, in the twitter section, SO much could have been said that wasn't. For instance, never send a DM unless you're following back, and Never link your twitter feed to Facebook because RT's # and @replies have no meaning in the Facebook feed. Much more was left out that really does need discussion about online manners.

I'd suggest a lot more focus in each area on manners and a lot less background info on each topic before final publication.

title: Vegan Dessert Cookbook: Recipes for Cakes, Cookies, Puddings, Candies, and More
text: This isn't the best match for me, but it's not a bad cookbook. While I am a full time vegetarian, I'm not a full time vegan. I'm trying to eat more plant based, though, in an attempt to heal my body from a health issue I have. So, when I eat vegan, it's for health reasons. I don't want a lot of expensive, and not so healthy vegan products in the recipes. Many of these use things like vegan butter(though it does say applesauce can be substituted for some of them), instant vegan pudding, vegan graham crackers, vegan chocolate chips, etc. I just want simple, healthy ingredients. There are also not many photos. That seems to be common in cookbooks these days, but I do still wish there were more. If you don't mind the extra products to have to buy, this cookbook could still work for you, but if you're like me and just want simple good food, this probably isn't the best pick.

title: Three Days in January: Dwight Eisenhower's Final Mission (Three Days Series)
text: This book was a gift for my husband, and he hasn’t read it yet.

title: Easy Origami (Dover Origami Papercraft)over 30 simple projects
text: This book is clearly geared toward kids. The basic projects can be enhanced by drawing faces on the animals, otherwise they are too simple. It's a good beginner's book.

title: Enchanted Forest: An Inky Quest and Coloring book (Activity Books, Mindfulness and Meditation, Illustrated Floral Prints)
text: This is a VERY beautiful coloring book!! I was super pleased to receive it and it is definitely a favorite of mine. My only wish is that the pages could be removed and the images did not extend into the binding of each page.  I wish All of this artist's books were designed with removable one -sided pages. I have seen some in her line that are but are priced differently, and of course do not have the same number of images that this book has. Regardless, they are all beautiful books and I will be ordering more in the future.

title: The Brightest Star in the Sky
text: Ha!  An unpredictible ending to a chick-lit book, who would have guessed?  Then again, I don't really want to bulk Marian Keyes' books with all the other chick-lit out there...in truth her books always deal with a much more serious subject...surrounded by the fun and romance of chick-lit.  This book follows the same pattern.  It's the story of the tenants in one building, who don't really know each other well, but come together at the end.  This confused group of people manage to find their ways through the book so that we have a happy ending.  As for the character telling the story, I was clueless until the end...

title: These Toxic Things: A Thriller
text: This is a nice, light read. Heavy on the suspense, light on romance, which is my preference for this genre.

title: The Rabbit Girls
text: This book makes you feel grateful, scared, sad, brave. I read long into the night and when it ended, I wanted to know more. What atrocities, what triumph!

title: Junior Encyclopedia of Animated Characters
text: We used this book to collect autographs at DisneyWorld. It was a bit heavy and bulky, but fit in my daughter's drawstring bag if she didn't have much else to carry. I carried it most days in my larger backpack. It is a nice keepsake and she was so excited to present it to characters for signing! The only character I remember coming across who wasn't featured was Baymax. He doesn't sign autographs though, so that worked out ok. There are not any characters from Big Hero 6 in the book. They do have over 150 characters in this book, many of whom are characters you can meet at the parks.

title: If the Magic Fits (100 Dresses)
text:
This is a sweet and fun story, and great for the middle grade group of fairy tale fans. The cover fits perfectly with the story. It's charming and whimsical. Even when Darling was in the under cellar scrubbing pots, I could still see the rainbows and butterflies outside.

This really is a great book for those that enjoy Jessica Day George and Shannon Hale, and I can easily recommend it to that group.

title: The Old Man and The Sea, Book Cover May Vary
text: The classic story of a fisherman.  My son had to read it for high school summer reading.  It's a short book, easily finished in one day - he packed it and took it to football camp, and read it before going to sleep over the course of a couple of nights.  I wish it had been available in hardback so it would last longer.

title: Breathe Your Last: An addictive and nail-biting crime thriller (Detective Josie Quinn Book 10)
text:
While on the Denton University campus to see her brother. Detective Josie Quinn discovers a body in the campus swimming pool. Those who know her are shocked when it’s revealed that Denton’s star swimmer Nysa Somers is a victim of drowning. There are no obvious signs of foul play and Josie’s chief wants the case closed. However, something about the young woman’s death doesn’t add up, and Josie just can’t let it go. When tragedy strikes again, nobody can deny there is a killer on the loose that Josie and her team need to stop before anyone else becomes a victim.

This book is the tenth Josie Quinn mystery. I started reading these books in the middle of the series, but have enjoyed each of the books I’ve read. This story is mainly told from Josie’s point of view with a few chapters told from the point of view as an unidentified killer who clearly feels justified in taking the lives of the victims. Josie is a complicated character with much tragedy in her personal life. She is trying to make a life with fellow detective Noah Fraley, but there are tensions between them as the case progresses.

This series combines the best of police procedurals and thrillers. The investigation is realistic and described in detail, and the tension builds as the story goes keeping the reader in suspense. No spoilers, but the details about the killing shock the Denton detectives but I’ve seen this method of killing in other books, so I thought someone on the team would have heard of it. That was my least favorite part of the otherwise suspenseful plot. The book is fast-paced and has some truly nail-biting moments toward the end of the book. I was pleased about how the book ends and can’t wait to see what’s in store for Josie in the future.

I received this ebook from NetGalley through the courtesy of Bookouture. An advance copy was provided to me at no cost, but my review is voluntary and unbiased.

title: One Second After (A John Matherson Novel, 1)
text: Scary prospects and with the current state of affairs in the world, pretty eye opening.  In a single chapter I was scared, sad, rooting for the good guys, and intrigued by what was coming next.

title: The 20th Victim (A Women's Murder Club Thriller)
text: The 19th Christmas and 20th Victim are both from the Women’s Murder Club series. It’s one of my favorites. The 19th was a very light read and not a normal one usually for this series. I think maybe because it seemed they were running around based on threats and not crimes it was odd. But we learned more about Joe and his past life before he married Lindsay so that was cool! The 20th I thought was fast paced and more like what I expect from these stories. In this one you have vigilante killers, and Lindsay clashes with her girls, because they are all on edge! I liked this one and I also like that their daughter Julie is growing up and part of the story now!

title: The Harvest Man (Scotland Yard's Murder Squad)
text:
I watched "Ripper Street" and "Secrets of Scotland Yard" on Netflix recently so my interest in Scotland Yard in the Victorian era has suddenly become a thing for me. I'm not so much interested in Jack the Ripper stories because that's a been-there-read-that kind of thing but it is absolutely fascinating how the Ripper murders, unsolved though they were, shaped Scotland Yard.

It was that mind-set that made me grab Alex Grecian's "The Harvest Man" as soon as it was available to read. I haven't read author Grecian's other novels and that's going to change. In fact, I'm going to re-read this book once I have more time because I feel like I read it way too fast and there is a lot that I missed.

"The Harvest Man" apparently picks up after the events of "The Devil's Workshop". The situations of Inspector Walter Day and his partner, Sergeant Nevil Hammersmith, are in flux. Both are injured and for Day, he is a husband and now a father to twins. Both have their worries and troubles that are legitimately based. However, just as Day is finally released to work in the field again, a new killer named The Harvest Man who kills couples and mutilates their faces needs to be caught.

There are a number of things that caught my attention and the first is how creepy Robert Louis Stevenson poetry can be and the other is that violence doesn't always have to be described in slathering detail but in spinetingling doses for greater impact. Grecian uses both to great effect as well as using the inter-relationships of the characters to build suspense as well. (The ending sets up perfectly for the next book in the series without needing an 'excerpt'.)

I now have "The Yard" on hold at the library as I feel like I'd probably get a greater appreciation of the characters. Day is a solid central character but I really like Hammersmith too. He and Day work well off each other even when they aren't working with each other. The dialogue is good; the pacing is brisk. Some aspects, like the vigilante group, I could have done without but it has a purpose so I can't ding it too much.

Definitely a series that I need to get into as this was a great introduction for me.

title: I Am a Bunny (A Golden Sturdy Book)
text: Adorable board book for very little readers or listeners with lovely pictures of the changing seasons.

title: Lockdown: A Novel of Suspense
text:
A shooting at a troubled California middle school has surprising consequences for a disparate cast of characters. The characters are introduced via flashbacks that lead up to the ill fated career day. They include the determined and cautiously optimistic principal, her English husband, a female police officer who was nearly killed by a gang banger, assorted students including a rich man's gun obsessed athlete son, the gang banger's cousin, a mysterious janitor and a stressed business man. the pace is fast and towards the end abbreviated chapters add to the suspense.

This is a readable if flawed book. It did hold my interest but there were way too many characters and none of them were especially well developed. It is at times overwritten with some florid prose. a subplot involving a missing sixth grader distracted and added nothing to the story. There are definitely too many characters. The previous disastrously violent Career Day does make oine wonder why they would engage in another, especially without greater precautions. However even though the reader is informed early on about what is going to happen it is presented with tension. Despite its flaws it keeps you reading. 3.5 stars

title: Clean Eating Kitchen: The Low-Carb Mediterranean Cookbook: Quick and Easy High-Protein, Low-Sugar, Healthy-Fat Recipes for Lifelong Health-More Than ... Meals to Prepare in 30 Minutes or Less
text:
An interesting take from a low carb perspective verses a ketogenic perspective. We, personally, have been practicing a ketogenic lifestyle for 3+ years for health and not weight loss reasons.

Good introduction from a whole real food perspective and not an 'anything goes" weight loss perspective and I appreciate that.

An average amount of pictures. Not too few or too many.

Some recipes I marked:
creamy spinach pie w/almond flour crust
lebanese meat pies
beef stew w/mushrooms & red wine

Nutritional information included at bottom of recipes.

Overall ... a fresh change from standard low carb recipes. Worth a look.

title: Tile Lock Scrabble,2 to 4 players
text:
I already own the [[ASIN:B00000IWH9 Scrabble Deluxe Turntable Game]] which I purchased on Amazon in the early 2000's for less than $50 (unlike the exorbitant $179.99 price listed as of this review).
